1244	% --- Supporting lemmas for the codomain read ---
1245	% These helpers establish that pullback along a scheme isomorphism is an equivalence
1246	% of module categories; the adjunction unit of an equivalence is an isomorphism,
1247	% used in the codomain-read computation.
1248	
1249	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1250	[Pullback along an isomorphism is an equivalence of module categories]
1251	  \label{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1252	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1253	  Let \(f : X \to Y\) be an isomorphism of schemes. Then the pullback functor on
1254	  \(\mathcal{O}\)-modules \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is
1255	  one half of an equivalence of categories
1256	  \(Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \simeq X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\), with quasi-inverse
1257	  \((f^{-1})^*\).
1258	\end{lemma}
1259	\begin{proof}
1260	
1261	  Assemble the equivalence from the functor \(f^*\) and the candidate inverse
1262	  \((f^{-1})^*\). The unit and counit isomorphisms are built from the pseudofunctor
1263	  coherences of pullback: \((f^{-1})^* \circ f^* \cong (f \circ f^{-1})^* = \mathrm{id}^*
1264	  \cong \mathrm{id}\) and symmetrically, using
1265	  \(f \circ f^{-1} = \mathrm{id}_Y\), \(f^{-1} \circ f = \mathrm{id}_X\)
1266	  (composition-of-pullbacks, congruence under equal maps, and pullback-of-identity).
1267	  The triangle identities are forced by these coherences.
1268	\end{proof}
1269	
1270	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1271	[The pullback functor along an isomorphism is an equivalence]
1272	  \label{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1273	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}
1274	  \uses{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso}
1275	  Consequently, for an isomorphism \(f : X \to Y\) the functor
1276	  \(f^* : Y\text{-}\mathbf{Mod} \to X\text{-}\mathbf{Mod}\) is an equivalence of
1277	  categories. In particular its adjunction unit is a natural isomorphism, so
1278	  \((\eta_{f})_{W}\) is invertible on every object \(W\).
1279	\end{lemma}
1280	\begin{proof}
1281	
1282	  The functor underlying the equivalence of
1283	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackIsoEquivalenceOfIso} is exactly \(f^*\), so \(f^*\) is an
1284	  equivalence; an equivalence's unit is a natural isomorphism.
1285	\end{proof}

1287	% --- Section-level identity, three pieces: regroup equiv (pure tensor algebra, native
1288	%     R'-linear via Algebra.IsPushout.cancelBaseChange) ← section identity (Γ(θ) is the
1289	%     R'-base change of the algebraic unit) ← IsIso corollary (the pinned leaf). ---
1290	
1291	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1292	[Regrouping isomorphism for the section-level mate]
1293	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1294	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}
1295	  % NOTE: tensor-order convention. The Lean uses Mathlib's `pullbackSpecIso`
1296	  % orientation `A ⊗[R] R'` (so the codomain is `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M` and the generator
1297	  % goes `r' ⊗ m ↦ (1 ⊗ r') ⊗ m`); the prose below writes `R' ⊗_R A`. The two are the
1298	  % same module up to `TensorProduct.comm` — a faithful re-orientation, no content change.
1299	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1300	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma
1301	  % `lemma-affine-base-change' (\S\,Cohomology and base change, I), proof, the
1302	  % "boils down to the equality" step (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex,
1303	  % L933--938).
1304	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Thus we see that the lemma boils down to the
1305	  % equality
1306	  % $$
1307	  % (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M = R' \otimes_R M
1308	  % $$
1309	  % as $R'$-modules."
1310	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base
1311	  change'' (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
1312	  Let \(\psi : R \to R'\) and \(\varphi : R \to A\) be ring homomorphisms and \(M\) an
1313	  \(A\)-module. There is a bundled \(R'\)-linear isomorphism
1314	  \[
1315	    \operatorname{regroup} :
1316	    (R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; R' \otimes_R M ,
1317	  \]
1318	  the ``equality as \(R'\)-modules'' of the source, sending the generator
1319	  \((r' \otimes 1) \otimes m \mapsto r' \otimes m\); equivalently its inverse sends
1320	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (r' \otimes 1) \otimes m\). It carries \emph{no} flatness
1321	  hypothesis.
1322	\end{lemma}
1323	
1324	\begin{proof}
1325	  \uses{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}
1326	  The underlying \(R'\)-linear equivalence is supplied, already as a bundled
1327	  \(\operatorname{LinearEquiv}\) over \(R'\), by the natively \(R'\)-linear
1328	  pushout-cancellation equivalence of
1329	  Lemma~\ref{lem:isPushout_cancelBaseChange_mathlib}. Crucially, that helper bundles
1330	  \(R'\)-linearity \emph{internally}: there is no hand-written scalar-compatibility
1331	  obligation, no induction over elementary tensors, and in particular \emph{no} residual
1332	  \(r' \cdot 0 = 0\) bookkeeping branch. The only work left to this proof is therefore to
1333	  promote that \(R'\)-linear equivalence to a morphism of \(R'\)-module \emph{objects} in
1334	  the exact extension/restriction-of-scalars packaging that the codomain and domain reads
1335	  produce. We work in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation
1336	  (the same module as \(R' \otimes_R A\) up to the canonical commutativity isomorphism,
1337	  per the tensor-order convention note).
1338	
1339	  \emph{The lone residual obstruction is the \(A\)-action diamond, resolved at the object
1340	  level.} The pushout-cancellation helper takes its relative tensor product over the
1341	  \emph{canonical} \(A\)-algebra structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\)
1342	  (multiplication in the left factor), whereas the carrier object produced by the
1343	  dictionaries,
1344	  the extension of scalars of \(M\) along the left inclusion \(\iota : A \to A \otimes_R R'\),
1345	  tensors over the \(A\)-module structure on \(A \otimes_R R'\) induced by \(\iota\). These
1346	  two \(A\)-module structures on
1347	  \(A \otimes_R R'\) agree on elements but are distinct as data, so the two relative
1348	  tensor products are distinct types even though their underlying abelian groups and
1349	  their \(R'\)-actions coincide. This diamond is the \emph{only} thing the helper does
1350	  not already discharge, and — because the \(R'\)-linearity is now entirely internal to
1351	  the Mathlib equivalence — it is resolved purely at the carrier level by an
1352	  identity-on-elements \(R'\)-linear bridge
1353	  \[
1354	    eT : (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\operatorname{restr}} M
1355	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
1356	    (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A^{\,\mathrm{can}} M ,
1357	  \]
1358	  the identity map on generators, which is \(R'\)-linear because both sides carry the
1359	  same \(R'\)-action (restriction of scalars along \(R' \to A \otimes_R R'\)).
1360	
1361	  Composing the bridge \(eT\) with the natively \(R'\)-linear helper equivalence and
1362	  promoting to an isomorphism of module objects yields the desired bundled
1363	  \(R'\)-linear isomorphism of \(\operatorname{ModuleCat}(R')\) objects. Being a composite
1364	  of linear equivalences it is an \(R'\)-linear isomorphism, and none of its constituents
1365	  carries a flatness hypothesis.
1366	\end{proof}

1663	\begin{lemma}

1667	  \label{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1668	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}
1669	  \uses{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib, lem:comp_unit_app_mathlib,
1670	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}

1673	  pullback--pushforward adjunction \((h^{*}\dashv h_{*})\) attached to a morphism \(h\), and
1674	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b}\), \(\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b}\) for the pseudofunctor
1675	  composition coherences. Then
1676	  \[
1677	    \eta^{b}_{N} \;\circ\; b_{*}\bigl(\eta^{a}_{\,b^{*}N}\bigr)
1678	      \;\circ\; (\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{hom}}_{N}
1679	    \;=\;
1680	    \eta^{a\circ b}_{N} \;\circ\;
1681	      (a\circ b)_{*}\bigl((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}_{N}\bigr),
1682	  \]
1683	  where composites are read left to right. In words: the unit of the composite morphism
1684	  \(a \circ b\), reindexed by the inverse pullback coherence, factors as the \(b\)-unit, followed
1685	  by \(b_{*}\) of the \(a\)-unit, followed by the pushforward coherence. This is the leg-reindex
1686	  engine consumed by Seam~2 (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_fstar_reindex}): with \(a\) an
1687	  isomorphism and \(b\) an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}\)-map it turns the generic projection-leg

1692	        lem:conjugateEquiv_pullbackComp_inv_mathlib}
1693	  Apply the unit-across-conjugate coherence Lemma~\ref{lem:unit_conjugateEquiv_mathlib} to the
1694	  composite adjunction \((b^{*}\dashv b_{*}) . (a^{*}\dashv a_{*})\), the adjunction
1695	  \(\bigl((a\circ b)^{*}\dashv (a\circ b)_{*}\bigr)\), and the left-adjoint transformation
1696	  \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\). This yields an identity whose right-hand factor
1697	  is the conjugate (mate) of \((\mathrm{pullbackComp}_{a,b})^{\mathrm{inv}}\); by

978	\subsubsection*{The internal grading of a homogeneous submodule (split $G_1$)}
979	
980	\begin{lemma}\leanok
981	[$G_1$ (independence) -- the family $n \mapsto M_n \cap p$ is independent]
982	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}
983	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_inf_iSupIndep}
984	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
985	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n M_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and let
986	  \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
987	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the family
988	  \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) of \(\kappa\)-subspaces of \(M\) is independent: for each
989	  \(n\),
990	  \[
991	    (M_n \cap p) \;\cap\; \Bigl(\bigsqcup_{m \ne n} (M_m \cap p)\Bigr) \;=\; 0 .
992	  \]
993	  The whole statement lives in the ambient module \(M\): the subspaces
994	  \(M_n \cap p\) are submodules of \(M\), never of a derived carrier \(\smash{\hat p}\).
995	\end{lemma}
996	
997	\begin{proof}
998	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
999	  \leanok
1000	  The components \(M_n\) of the internal decomposition of \(M\) are independent. The
1001	  subfamily \(n \mapsto M_n \cap p\) consists of submodules with \(M_n \cap p \le M_n\),
1002	  so independence is inherited: a relation among the \(M_n \cap p\) is in particular a
1003	  relation among the \(M_n\), forcing each term to vanish.
1004	\end{proof}
1005	
1006	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1007	[$G_1$ (supremum) -- $\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) = p$ for $p$ homogeneous]
1008	  \label{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1009	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_inf_eq}
1010	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1011	  Let \(p \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1012	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then
1013	  \[
1014	    \bigsqcup_{n} (M_n \cap p) \;=\; p .
1015	  \]
1016	  Together with the independence of
1017	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}, this is the ambient internal
1018	  direct sum \(p = \bigoplus_n (M_n \cap p)\), stated entirely inside \(M\) without
1019	  requiring a direct-sum decomposition of the subtype \(\smash{\hat p}\).
1020	\end{lemma}
1021	
1022	\begin{proof}
1023	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1024	  \leanok
1025	  The inclusion \(\bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p) \le p\) is clear, since each
1026	  \(M_n \cap p \le p\). For the reverse inclusion, take \(m \in p\) and write
1027	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n\) using the
1028	  internal decomposition of \(M\). Homogeneity of \(p\) gives
1029	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in p\), hence \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in M_n \cap p\); thus
1030	  \(m\) is a finite sum of elements of the \(M_n \cap p\), i.e.
1031	  \(m \in \bigsqcup_n (M_n \cap p)\).
1032	\end{proof}

1034	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient class and its Hilbert function}
1035	
1036	\begin{definition}\leanok
1037	[Subquotient Hilbert data]
1038	  \label{def:graded_subquotientHilb}
1039	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotientHilb}
1040	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum}
1041	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.hilb}
1042	  \uses{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1043	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}
1044	  Fix a graded \(\kappa\)-module \(\mathcal{M}\), i.e.\ \(M\) together with an
1045	  internal decomposition \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) whose components
1046	  \(\mathcal{M}_n\) are finite-dimensional over \(\kappa\). A \emph{subquotient
1047	  datum of length \(r\)} consists of:
1048	  \begin{itemize}
1049	    \item a pair of homogeneous submodules \(N' \le N \subseteq M\); and
1050	    \item a family \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to (M \to_\kappa M)\) of pairwise
1051	      commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms, each raising degree by one
1052	      (\(t_i(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\)), each preserving \(N\)
1053	      and \(N'\) (\(t_i N \subseteq N\), \(t_i N' \subseteq N'\)); together with
1054	    \item a finiteness condition: the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a finite module over the
1055	      polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) acting through the \(t_i\) (via the
1056	      ambient \(t\)-stable carriers \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N\),
1057	      \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,N'\) of \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}).
1058	  \end{itemize}
1059	  The pair \((N, N')\) represents the subquotient \(N/N'\). Its \emph{ambient
1060	  Hilbert function} is
1061	  \[
1062	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \Bigl(\dim_\kappa (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
1063	      \;-\; \dim_\kappa (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\Bigr) \;\in\; \mathbb{Q},
1064	  \]
1065	  the difference of the (finite) \(\kappa\)-dimensions of the ambient intersections,
1066	  computed in \(\mathbb{Z}\) and cast to \(\mathbb{Q}\). Since \(N' \le N\) one has
1067	  \(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so
1068	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = \dim_\kappa\bigl((N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\bigr)\)
1069	  is the dimension of the degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\). The split \(G_1\)
1070	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep},
1071	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) guarantees that these ambient
1072	  intersections assemble the homogeneous submodules \(N, N'\), so the data depends
1073	  only on the ambient families \(n \mapsto N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\),
1074	  \(n \mapsto N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\).
1075	\end{definition}
1076	
1077	\subsubsection*{Ambient homogeneity calculus}
1078	
1079	The kernel/cokernel closure and the degreewise difference identity rest on a small
1080	calculus of how a degree-raising endomorphism interacts with the internal grading and
1081	with the lattice of homogeneous submodules. Throughout, \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\)
1082	is the fixed internally graded \(\kappa\)-module and \(x : M \to M\) is a
1083	\(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism raising degree by one. None of these statements forms a
1084	derived carrier: every object is a submodule of the fixed \(M\).
1085	
1086	\begin{definition}\leanok
1087	[Degree-raising endomorphism]
1088	  \label{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1089	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree}
1090	  Let \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) be an internally graded \(\kappa\)-module. A
1091	  \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphism \(x : M \to M\) \emph{raises degree by one} when it
1092	  carries each graded piece into the next: \(x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1093	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) for every \(n\). This is the grading-ring-free form of
1094	  ``multiplication by a degree-one element'': it abstracts each of the \(r\)
1095	  commuting degree-one generators \(x \in R_1\) acting on \(M\) into a single
1096	  endomorphism, with no reference to the grading ring \(R\).
1097	\end{definition}
1098	
1099	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1100	[Membership form of degree-raising]
1101	  \label{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1102	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.RaisesDegree.mem}
1103	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1104	  If \(x\) raises degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and \(m \in
1105	  \mathcal{M}_n\), then \(x m \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1106	\end{lemma}
1107	
1108	\begin{proof}
1109	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1110	  \leanok
1111	  Immediate from the definition: \(x m \in x(\mathcal{M}_n) \subseteq
1112	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\).
1113	\end{proof}
1114	
1115	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1116	[Degree-shift commutation]
1117	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1118	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree}
1119	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1120	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then for every \(m \in M\) and every \(i\) the
1121	  degree-\((i+1)\) homogeneous component of \(x m\) equals \(x\) applied to the
1122	  degree-\(i\) component of \(m\):
1123	  \[
1124	    \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) \;=\; x\bigl(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)\bigr) .
1125	  \]
1126	  This load-bearing commutation says a degree-raising endomorphism shifts the
1127	  internal decomposition by exactly one slot; it is what makes preimages and images
1128	  of homogeneous submodules under \(x\) homogeneous.
1129	\end{lemma}
1130	
1131	\begin{proof}
1132	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree, lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}
1133	  \leanok
1134	  Decompose \(m = \sum_j \mathrm{proj}_j(m)\) into its finitely many homogeneous
1135	  components and apply \(x\): \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with
1136	  \(x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m)) \in \mathcal{M}_{j+1}\) by
1137	  \cref{lem:graded_raisesDegree_mem}. Reading off the degree-\((i+1)\) component, all
1138	  terms with \(j+1 \ne i+1\) drop out, leaving \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\).
1139	\end{proof}
1140	
1141	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1142	[Degree-raising kills the bottom]
1143	  \label{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1144	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1145	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1146	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one. Then the degree-\(0\) component of \(x m\) vanishes
1147	  for every \(m\): \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0\).
1148	\end{lemma}
1149	
1150	\begin{proof}
1151	  \uses{def:graded_raisesDegree}
1152	  \leanok
1153	  Writing \(x m = \sum_j x(\mathrm{proj}_j(m))\) with each summand in
1154	  \(\mathcal{M}_{j+1}\), no summand has degree \(0\) (every \(j+1 \ge 1\)), so the
1155	  degree-\(0\) component is \(0\).
1156	\end{proof}
1157	
1158	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1159	[Preimage of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1160	  \label{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1161	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_isHomogeneous}
1162	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1163	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N' \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule
1164	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}). Then the preimage \(x^{-1}(N') =
1165	  \{m : x m \in N'\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of \(M\).
1166	\end{lemma}
1167	
1168	\begin{proof}
1169	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}
1170	  \leanok
1171	  Let \(m \in x^{-1}(N')\) and fix a degree \(i\). By the degree-shift commutation
1172	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}), \(x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m)) =
1173	  \mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m)\), which lies in \(N'\) because \(x m \in N'\) and \(N'\)
1174	  is homogeneous. Hence \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\), so \(x^{-1}(N')\) is
1175	  homogeneous.
1176	\end{proof}
1177	
1178	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1179	[Image of a homogeneous submodule is homogeneous]
1180	  \label{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1181	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_isHomogeneous}
1182	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1183	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1184	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be a homogeneous submodule.
1185	  Then the image \(x \cdot N = \{x m : m \in N\}\) is a homogeneous submodule of
1186	  \(M\), with degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(x(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) and zero
1187	  degree-\(0\) piece.
1188	\end{lemma}
1189	
1190	\begin{proof}
1191	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib, lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree,
1192	    lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}
1193	    \leanok
1194	  Take \(x m \in x \cdot N\) with \(m \in N\) and fix a degree. In degree \(0\) the
1195	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_0(x m) = 0 \in x \cdot N\)
1196	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree_zero}). In degree \(i+1\),
1197	  \(\mathrm{proj}_{i+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_i(m))\)
1198	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); since \(N\) is homogeneous,
1199	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in N\), so this component lies in \(x \cdot N\). Thus every
1200	  homogeneous component of \(x m\) lies in \(x \cdot N\), which is therefore
1201	  homogeneous.
1202	\end{proof}
1203	
1204	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1205	[Intersection of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1206	  \label{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}
1207	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.inf_isHomogeneous}
1208	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1209	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules
1210	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) then so is their intersection \(p
1211	  \cap q\). Mathlib provides no lattice-closure lemmas for homogeneous submodules, so
1212	  this is recorded here.
1213	\end{lemma}
1214	
1215	\begin{proof}
1216	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1217	  \leanok
1218	  For \(m \in p \cap q\) and any degree \(i\), homogeneity of \(p\) and of \(q\)
1219	  gives \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in q\), hence
1220	  \(\mathrm{proj}_i(m) \in p \cap q\).
1221	\end{proof}
1222	
1223	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1224	[Sum of homogeneous submodules is homogeneous]
1225	  \label{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1226	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_isHomogeneous}
1227	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1228	  If \(p, q \subseteq M\) are homogeneous submodules then so is their sum \(p + q\).
1229	\end{lemma}
1230	
1231	\begin{proof}
1232	  \uses{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1233	  \leanok
1234	  Write an element of \(p + q\) as \(a + b\) with \(a \in p\), \(b \in q\). For any
1235	  degree \(i\), \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) = \mathrm{proj}_i(a) + \mathrm{proj}_i(b)\)
1236	  with \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a) \in p\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_i(b) \in q\) by homogeneity,
1237	  so \(\mathrm{proj}_i(a+b) \in p + q\).
1238	\end{proof}
1239	
1240	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1241	[Ambient image identity]
1242	  \label{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}
1243	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_inf_degree_eq}
1244	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1245	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one and let \(N \subseteq M\) be homogeneous. Then the
1246	  degree-\((n+1)\) part of the image \(x \cdot N\) is exactly \(x\) of the
1247	  degree-\(n\) part of \(N\):
1248	  \[
1249	    (x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \;=\; x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) .
1250	  \]
1251	\end{lemma}
1252	
1253	\begin{proof}
1254	  \uses{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1255	  \leanok
1256	  \(\supseteq\): for \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), \(x m \in x \cdot N\) and \(x m
1257	  \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) since \(x\) raises degree. \(\subseteq\): an element \(x m
1258	  \in x \cdot N\) lying in \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) equals its own degree-\((n+1)\)
1259	  component \(\mathrm{proj}_{n+1}(x m) = x(\mathrm{proj}_n(m))\)
1260	  (\cref{lem:graded_decompose_raisesDegree}); homogeneity of \(N\) puts
1261	  \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\), so \(x m \in x \cdot (N \cap
1262	  \mathcal{M}_n)\). The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous by
1263	  \cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}.
1264	\end{proof}
1265	
1266	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1267	[Ambient distributive law]
1268	  \label{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.sup_inf_degree_eq}
1270	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1271	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1272	  For homogeneous submodules \(P, Q \subseteq M\) and any degree \(k\), intersecting
1273	  the sum with a graded piece distributes:
1274	  \[
1275	    (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k \;=\; (P \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k) .
1276	  \]
1277	\end{lemma}
1278	
1279	\begin{proof}
1280	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous,
1281	    lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}
1282	    \leanok
1283	  \(\supseteq\) is the general lattice inequality. For \(\subseteq\), take \(z = p +
1284	  q \in (P + Q) \cap \mathcal{M}_k\) with \(p \in P\), \(q \in Q\). Since \(z \in
1285	  \mathcal{M}_k\), \(z = \mathrm{proj}_k(z) = \mathrm{proj}_k(p) +
1286	  \mathrm{proj}_k(q)\); homogeneity of \(P\) and \(Q\)
1287	  (\cref{lem:submodule_isHomogeneous_mathlib}) puts \(\mathrm{proj}_k(p) \in P \cap
1288	  \mathcal{M}_k\) and \(\mathrm{proj}_k(q) \in Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k\), so \(z \in (P
1289	  \cap \mathcal{M}_k) + (Q \cap \mathcal{M}_k)\). The intersection and sum stay in
1290	  the homogeneous lattice by \cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous} and
1291	  \cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}.
1292	\end{proof}

1294	\subsubsection*{Closure under kernel and cokernel}
1295	
1296	The kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class is realised by eight ambient
1297	component facts: for a degree-raising endomorphism \(x\) and a homogeneous pair
1298	\(N' \le N\), the kernel pair is \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel pair is
1299	\((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\). The lemmas below record that both new pairs are homogeneous,
1300	nest correctly, are annihilated by \(x\), and are preserved by any endomorphism \(t\)
1301	commuting with \(x\) that preserves the original pair.
1302	
1303	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1304	[Homogeneity of the kernel pair's lower module]
1305	  \label{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}
1306	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_isHomogeneous}
1307	  \uses{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}
1308	  Let \(x\) raise degree by one (\cref{def:graded_raisesDegree}) and let \(N, N'\) be
1309	  homogeneous submodules of \(M\). Then the kernel pair's lower module
1310	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous. Indeed it is the intersection
1311	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous \(N\) with the homogeneous
1312	  preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}).
1313	\end{lemma}
1314	
1315	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1316	[Homogeneity of the cokernel pair's upper module]
1317	  \label{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}
1318	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_isHomogeneous}
1319	  \uses{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}
1320	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1321	  homogeneous: it is the sum (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}) of the homogeneous
1322	  \(N'\) with the homogeneous image \(x \cdot N\)
1323	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}).
1324	\end{lemma}
1325	
1326	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1327	[The kernel pair nests]
1328	  \label{lem:graded_ker_le}
1329	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_le}
1330	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N'\) (\(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)), then
1331	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed \(N' \le N\) by hypothesis, and \(x \cdot N'
1332	  \subseteq N'\) is exactly \(N' \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\); so \(N'\) is below the
1333	  intersection. Hence the kernel pair \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) is a valid
1334	  subquotient pair (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1335	\end{lemma}
1336	
1337	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1338	[The cokernel pair nests]
1339	  \label{lem:graded_coker_le}
1340	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_le}
1341	  If \(N' \le N\) and \(x\) preserves \(N\) (\(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)), then
1342	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\): both summands lie in \(N\). Hence the cokernel pair
1343	  \((N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) is a valid subquotient pair
1344	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}).
1345	\end{lemma}
1346	
1347	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1348	[$x$ annihilates the kernel subquotient]
1349	  \label{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}
1350	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_annihilate}
1351	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries the kernel pair's lower module into \(N'\):
1352	  \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\). Indeed \(N \cap x^{-1}(N') \subseteq
1353	  x^{-1}(N')\), and applying \(x\) to a preimage of \(N'\) lands in \(N'\). Thus \(x\)
1354	  acts as zero on the kernel subquotient.
1355	\end{lemma}
1356	
1357	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1358	[$x$ annihilates the cokernel subquotient]
1359	  \label{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}
1360	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_annihilate}
1361	  The endomorphism \(x\) carries \(N\) into the cokernel pair's upper module:
1362	  \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\), trivially (the right summand). Thus \(x\)
1363	  acts as zero on the cokernel subquotient \(N/(N' + x \cdot N)\).
1364	\end{lemma}
1365	
1366	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1367	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the preimage]
1368	  \label{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1369	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.comap_map_le_of_commute}
1370	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N'\) (\(t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)). Then
1371	  \(t\) preserves the preimage: \(t \cdot x^{-1}(N') \subseteq x^{-1}(N')\). Indeed for
1372	  \(m\) with \(x m \in N'\) one has \(x(t m) = t(x m) \in t \cdot N' \subseteq N'\), so
1373	  \(t m \in x^{-1}(N')\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the kernel
1374	  pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\).
1375	\end{lemma}
1376	
1377	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1378	[Commuting endomorphisms preserve the image]
1379	  \label{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1380	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.map_map_le_of_commute}
1381	  Let \(t\) commute with \(x\) and preserve \(N\) (\(t \cdot N \subseteq N\)). Then
1382	  \(t\) preserves the image: \(t \cdot (x \cdot N) \subseteq x \cdot N\). Indeed for
1383	  \(m \in N\) one has \(t(x m) = x(t m)\) with \(t m \in N\), so \(t(x m) \in x \cdot
1384	  N\). This is what shows the remaining \(t_i\) preserve the cokernel pair's upper
1385	  module \(N' + x \cdot N\).
1386	\end{lemma}
1387	
1388	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1389	[Kernel/cokernel closure of the subquotient class]
1390	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}
1391	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1392	  % This is a narrative grouping lemma: its content is realised in Lean by the eight
1393	  % ambient component facts cited in \uses{} below (no single Lean declaration),
1394	  % together with the SubquotientDatum.ker/.coker constructors that bundle them into a
1395	  % length-(r-1) datum. The `\lean` pin names the `.ker` constructor — the single decl most
1396	  % representative of the bundled closure — so this dependency-bearing grouping node (which is
1397	  % `\uses`'d by the keystone rationality induction) stays wired into the graph rather than
1398	  % orphaning the component facts it `\uses`. This is a deliberate duplicate of the
1399	  % `def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker` pin.
1400	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1401	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1402	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1403	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1404	    def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1405	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1406	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1407	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1408	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1409	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1410	  endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\), and set \(x = t_{r-1}\). Form the two new
1411	  pairs of homogeneous submodules of \(M\)
1412	  \[
1413	    K \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr),
1414	    \qquad
1415	    C \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr),
1416	  \]
1417	  where \(x^{-1}(N') = \{\,m \in M : x \cdot m \in N'\,\}\) is the preimage and
1418	  \(x \cdot N\) is the image of \(N\) under \(x\). Then:
1419	  \begin{enumerate}
1420	    \item \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N\) are homogeneous submodules of
1421	      \(M\), with \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) and \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\), so both
1422	      \(K\) and \(C\) are subquotient pairs;
1423	    \item the remaining endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\) pairwise commute,
1424	      raise degree by one, and preserve both members of \(K\) and of \(C\); and
1425	    \item \(x\) annihilates both subquotients: \(x \cdot (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \subseteq N'\)
1426	      and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\).
1427	  \end{enumerate}
1428	  Consequently \(K\) and \(C\) are again subquotient data, now of length \(r-1\)
1429	  (the endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}\)), and \(K = \ker x\) and \(C =
1430	  \operatorname{coker} x\) on the subquotient \(N/N'\): \(K\) represents
1431	  \(\ker\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\) and \(C\) represents
1432	  \(\operatorname{coker}\bigl(x : N/N' \to N/N'\bigr)\). No quotient or subtype
1433	  carrier is constructed; \(K\) and \(C\) are ambient pairs of homogeneous
1434	  submodules of the fixed \(M\).
1435	\end{lemma}
1436	
1437	\begin{proof}
1438	  \uses{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous,
1439	    lem:graded_ker_le, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_ker_annihilate,
1440	    lem:graded_coker_annihilate, lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute,
1441	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute,
1442	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1443	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib,
1444	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous,
1445	    lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous,
1446	    lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}
1447	    \leanok
1448	  \emph{Homogeneity.} The preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous: if
1449	  \(m = \sum_n \mathrm{proj}_n(m)\) with \(x \cdot m \in N'\), then since \(x\) raises
1450	  degree by one (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) the components
1451	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) lie in distinct degrees, and
1452	  homogeneity of \(N'\) (split \(G_1\),
1453	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) forces each
1454	  \(x \cdot \mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in N'\), i.e.\ \(\mathrm{proj}_n(m) \in x^{-1}(N')\).
1455	  Thus \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is an intersection of homogeneous submodules, hence
1456	  homogeneous. The image \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous with degree-\((n+1)\) piece
1457	  \(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) by
1458	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}, and a sum of homogeneous
1459	  submodules is homogeneous (\cref{lem:ideal_homogeneous_span_mathlib} applied at the
1460	  module level), so \(N' + x \cdot N\) is homogeneous. The inclusions
1461	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (as \(x \cdot N' \subseteq N'\)) and
1462	  \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (as \(N' \le N\) and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N\)) are
1463	  immediate. In the language of the ambient homogeneity calculus, this paragraph is
1464	  exactly: \(x^{-1}(N')\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_comap_isHomogeneous}),
1465	  \(x \cdot N\) is homogeneous (\cref{lem:graded_map_isHomogeneous}), and the
1466	  homogeneous lattice is closed under intersection
1467	  (\cref{lem:graded_inf_isHomogeneous}) and sum
1468	  (\cref{lem:graded_sup_isHomogeneous}).
1469	
1470	  \emph{Preservation.} Each \(t_i\) with \(i \le r-2\) commutes with \(x\) and
1471	  preserves \(N, N'\); hence it preserves \(x^{-1}(N')\) (if \(x t_i m = t_i x m \in
1472	  N'\) whenever \(x m \in N'\)), so it preserves \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\), and it
1473	  preserves \(x \cdot N\) (as \(t_i x N = x t_i N \subseteq x N\)), so it preserves
1474	  \(N' + x \cdot N\). Degree-raising and commutativity are inherited.
1475	
1476	  \emph{Annihilation by \(x\).} For \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) one has
1477	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\) by definition, so \(x\) carries the first member of \(K\) into
1478	  its second; and \(x \cdot N \subseteq N' + x \cdot N\) trivially. Thus \(x\) acts as
1479	  zero on \(N/N'\) restricted to \(K\) and on the cokernel quotient \(C\).
1480	
1481	  \emph{Identification with \(\ker x\) and \(\operatorname{coker} x\).} On the
1482	  subquotient \(N/N'\), the class of \(m \in N\) is killed by \(x\) iff
1483	  \(x \cdot m \in N'\), i.e.\ iff \(m \in N \cap x^{-1}(N')\); so \(\ker(x : N/N' \to
1484	  N/N')\) is exactly \((N \cap x^{-1}(N'))/N'\), represented by \(K\). The image of
1485	  \(x\) on \(N/N'\) is \((N' + x \cdot N)/N'\), so the cokernel
1486	  \((N/N')/\operatorname{im} x = N/(N' + x \cdot N)\) is represented by \(C\).
1487	\end{proof}
1488	
1489	\subsubsection*{The subquotient degreewise difference identity ($D_6$)}
1490	
1491	\begin{lemma}
1492	
1493	\leanok
1494	[preimage dimension equals ambient-intersection dimension]
1495	  \label{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype}
1496	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finrank_comap_subtype}
1497	  For submodules \(p, S\) of a \(\kappa\)-vector space \(M\), the dimension of the
1498	  preimage of \(S\) under the inclusion \(p \hookrightarrow M\) equals the dimension of
1499	  the ambient intersection: \(\dim_\kappa(\iota_p^{-1} S) = \dim_\kappa(p \cap S)\),
1500	  where \(\iota_p : p \to M\) is the canonical injection. Project-local helper consumed
1501	  by the two kernel-dimension computations of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}.
1502	\end{lemma}
1503	\begin{proof}
1504	
1505	\leanok
1506	  The inclusion \(\iota_p\) is injective, so it restricts to a \(\kappa\)-linear
1507	  isomorphism from \(\iota_p^{-1} S\) onto its image \(\iota_p(\iota_p^{-1} S) = p \cap S\)
1508	  (image of a comap under an injective map). An isomorphism preserves dimension, giving
1509	  the claimed equality.
1510	\end{proof}
1511	
1512	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1513	[$D_6$ -- subquotient degreewise difference]
1514	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1515	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_degreewise_diff}
1516	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
1517	    lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff, lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
1518	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq, lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype,
1519	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1520	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1521	  % NOTE: The proof factors the two kernel-dimension computations through the
1522	  %   helper \cref{lem:graded_finrank_comap_subtype} (preimage dimension = ambient
1523	  %   intersection dimension).
1524	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
1525	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
1526	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1527	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (degreewise short exact sequence).}
1528	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with
1529	  Hilbert function \(h_M\), and let \(x = t_{r-1}\). Let \(h_K\) and \(h_C\) be the
1530	  Hilbert functions of the kernel and cokernel subquotients
1531	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) of
1532	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}. Then for every \(n\)
1533	  \[
1534	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1535	  \]
1536	  Writing \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) for the
1537	  degree-\(n\) piece of \(N/N'\) (so \(\dim_\kappa Q_n = h_M(n)\)), multiplication by
1538	  \(x\) is a \(\kappa\)-linear map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) with
1539	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\) and
1540	  \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x\) the degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\); the identity is
1541	  then the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1542	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}). Specialising to \((N, N') = (\top,
1543	  \bot)\) recovers the classical \(0 \to \mathcal{M}_n \xrightarrow{x}
1544	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x\mathcal{M}_n \to 0\).
1545	\end{lemma}
1546	
1547	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, inductive step (read
1548	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13939-L13947).
1549	% "Let $\overline{M} = M/xM$. Note that the map $x : M \to M$
1550	%  is {\it not} a map of graded $S$-modules, since it does
1551	%  not map $M_d$ into $M_d$. Namely, for each $d$ we have the
1552	%  following short exact sequence
1553	%  $$
1554	%  0 \to M_d \xrightarrow{x} M_{d + 1} \to \overline{M}_{d + 1} \to 0
1555	%  $$
1556	%  This proves that $[M_{d + 1}] - [M_d] = [\overline{M}_{d + 1}]$.
1557	%  Hence we win by Lemma \ref{lemma-numerical-polynomial}."
1558	\begin{proof}
1559	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker, lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff,
1560	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq,
1561	    lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib, lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq,
1562	    lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq}
1563	    \leanok
1564	  Since \(N' \le N\) are homogeneous, the split \(G_1\)
1565	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq}) identifies the degree-\(n\) piece
1566	  of \(N/N'\) with \(Q_n = (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\), a
1567	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space of dimension \(h_M(n)\). Because \(x\)
1568	  raises degree by one and preserves \(N\) and \(N'\)
1569	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}), it induces a \(\kappa\)-linear
1570	  map \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\).
1571	
1572	  \emph{Kernel.} A class \([m] \in Q_n\) (with \(m \in N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)) lies in
1573	  \(\ker(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\) iff \(x \cdot m \in N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\), i.e.\
1574	  iff \(m \in (N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\). Hence \(\ker(x : Q_n \to
1575	  Q_{n+1}) = \bigl((N \cap x^{-1}(N')) \cap \mathcal{M}_n\bigr)/(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1576	  is exactly the degree-\(n\) piece of \(K\), of dimension \(h_K(n)\)
1577	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}).
1578	
1579	  \emph{Cokernel.} The image of \(x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1}\) is
1580	  \(\bigl(x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n) + (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\bigr)/(N' \cap
1581	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\). Using the ambient identity \((N' + x \cdot N) \cap
1582	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = (N' \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}) + x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1583	  -- which is the ambient distributive law (\cref{lem:graded_sup_inf_degree_eq})
1584	  applied to \(P = N'\), \(Q = x \cdot N\), combined with the ambient image identity
1585	  \((x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1} = x \cdot (N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\)
1586	  (\cref{lem:graded_map_inf_degree_eq}) -- the cokernel \(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im} x = (N \cap
1587	  \mathcal{M}_{n+1})/\bigl((N' + x \cdot N) \cap \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\bigr)\) is the
1588	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece of \(C\), of dimension \(h_C(n+1)\).
1589	
1590	  \emph{Difference.} Apply the degreewise rank--nullity difference \(D_5\)
1591	  (\cref{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}) to \(\varphi = (x : Q_n \to Q_{n+1})\),
1592	  with \(V = Q_n\), \(W = Q_{n+1}\):
1593	  \[
1594	    h_M(n+1) - h_M(n)
1595	      = \dim_\kappa Q_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa Q_n
1596	      = \dim_\kappa(Q_{n+1}/\operatorname{im}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)
1597	      = h_C(n+1) - h_K(n).
1598	  \]
1599	  This is the \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) hypothesis consumed by \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}.
1600	\end{proof}
1601	
1602	\subsubsection*{The free polynomial-ring module structure}
1603	
1604	The finiteness condition of a subquotient datum (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) and
1605	its transfer down one generator (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1606	phrased over the \emph{free} polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] =
1607	\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0, \dots, r-1\}, \kappa)\). The following blocks build the
1608	action of that ring on the ambient module \(M\) from the \(r\) pairwise-commuting
1609	degree-raising endomorphisms \(t_i\), keeping every carrier an ambient submodule of
1610	\(M\). The free polynomial ring -- rather than the subalgebra
1611	\(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\)
1612	-- is used precisely so the inductive transfer has the ring surjection
1613	\(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-2}]\) available
1614	(\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}).
1615	
1616	\begin{definition}\leanok
1617	[Polynomial evaluation ring homomorphism]
1618	  \label{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1619	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom}
1620	  \uses{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib}
1621	  Let \(t : \{0, \dots, r-1\} \to \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) be a family of
1622	  pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms. The \emph{polynomial evaluation
1623	  ring homomorphism}
1624	  \[
1625	    \mathrm{polyEndHom}(t) : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \longrightarrow
1626	      \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)
1627	  \]
1628	  sends each variable \(X_i\) to \(t_i\). It is constructed by evaluating into the
1629	  \emph{commutative} \(\kappa\)-subalgebra \(A = \mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa(\{t_i\})
1630	  \subseteq \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) -- commutative by
1631	  \cref{lem:adjoinCommRingOfComm_mathlib} because the generators commute -- and then
1632	  including \(A \hookrightarrow \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\); evaluation into the
1633	  noncommutative \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) directly is not an algebra
1634	  homomorphism, which is why the commutative \(A\) is interposed.
1635	\end{definition}
1636	
1637	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1638	[Evaluation sends $X_i$ to $t_i$]
1639	  \label{lem:graded_polyEndHom_X}
1640	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.polyEndHom_X}
1641	  \uses{def:graded_polyEndHom}
1642	  \(\mathrm{polyEndHom}(t)(X_i) = t_i\) for each \(i\). Immediate from the construction
1643	  (\cref{def:graded_polyEndHom}), as evaluation sends \(X_i\) to the chosen image
1644	  \(t_i\).
1645	\end{lemma}
1646	
1647	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1648	[Evaluation sends a constant to a scalar]

1730	\subsubsection*{Finiteness-transfer infrastructure}
1731	
1732	The ambient finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer})
1733	factors the polynomial action of the larger ring through a surjection onto the
1734	smaller one. The following blocks build that surjection and the semilinearity
1735	identity it rests on, and record the two elementary finiteness manipulations
1736	(shrinking a numerator, enlarging a denominator) used to feed the kernel and
1737	cokernel constructors.
1738	
1739	\begin{definition}
1740	
1741	\leanok
1742	[The last-variable surjection of polynomial rings]
1743	  \label{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}
1744	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom}
1745	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_castSucc}
1746	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_X_last}
1747	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_C}
1748	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_rename_castSucc}
1749	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_surjective}
1750	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.lastVarAlgHom_ringHomSurjective}
1751	  \uses{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1752	  For a field \(\kappa\) and \(r \in \mathbb{N}\), the \emph{last-variable
1753	  homomorphism} is the \(\kappa\)-algebra map
1754	  \[
1755	    \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1756	      = \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r\}, \kappa)
1757	      \;\longrightarrow\;
1758	      \operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\{0,\dots,r-1\}, \kappa) = \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]
1759	  \]
1760	  obtained by evaluation: it sends the last variable \(X_r\) to \(0\) and each
1761	  earlier variable \(X_i\) (for \(0 \le i < r\)) to \(X_i\), and fixes constants.
1762	  It is a left inverse of the algebra map
1763	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}] \to \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1764	  induced by the canonical inclusion \(\{0, \dots, r-1\} \hookrightarrow \{0, \dots, r\}\)
1765	  on variable indices, and is therefore surjective. This is the concrete
1766	  ring surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1767	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) along which finiteness is transferred
1768	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}).
1769	\end{definition}

2081	\begin{proof}

2083	  \leanok
2084	  The products \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), as \(I, J\) range over the
2085	  size-\(d\) subsets, form an affine open cover of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
2086	  \times_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Separatedness may be checked on this
2087	  cover: it suffices to show that for each pair \((I,J)\) the preimage
2088	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J)\) maps by a closed immersion into
2089	  \(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\), i.e.\ that the corresponding ring
2090	  homomorphism is surjective.
2091	
2092	  By \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme} the diagonal preimage is the overlap
2093	  \(\Delta^{-1}(U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J) = U^I \cap U^J = U^I_J = \Spec
2094	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\). The restricted diagonal corresponds to the ring map
2095	  \[
2096	    \delta_{I,J} : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \otimes_{\mathbb{Z}} \mathbb{Z}[X^J]
2097	    \longrightarrow \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J],
2098	    \qquad
2099	    X^I \otimes 1 \mapsto X^I, \quad 1 \otimes X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I,

2101	  the second component being the comorphism \(\theta_{I,J}\) of
2102	  \cref{def:gr_transition} (a point of \(U^I_J\) and its image under
2103	  \(\theta_{I,J}\) are the two coordinates of a point of the diagonal). We claim
2104	  \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Its image contains all entries of \(X^I\)
2105	  (from the first factor). From the second factor it contains the entries of
2106	  \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\); taking the \(I\)-minor of this matrix, and using
2107	  \(X^I_I = 1_{d\times d}\), the image contains the entries of \((X^I_J)^{-1}\),
2108	  and in particular
2109	  \[
2110	    \delta_{I,J}(1 \otimes P^J_I) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr) = 1/P^I_J .
2111	  \]
2112	  Hence \(1/P^I_J\) lies in the image, so the image is all of
2113	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) and \(\delta_{I,J}\) is surjective. Concretely the
2114	  closed subscheme \(\Delta_{I,J} \subseteq U^I \times_{\mathbb{Z}} U^J\) is cut
2115	  out by the entries of the matrix relation
2116	  \[
2117	    X^J_I \, X^I - X^J \;=\; 0,
2118	  \]

2121	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I = X^J\). As each restricted diagonal is a closed immersion,
2122	  \(\Delta\) is a closed immersion and \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is separated over
2123	  \(\mathbb{Z}\).

2126	\section{Properness}
2127	\label{sec:gr_proper}
2128	
2129	Following Nitsure \S 1, the structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec
2130	\mathbb{Z}\) is proper. We reduce properness to the \emph{existence} half of the
2131	valuative criterion: the Mathlib criterion \texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion}
2132	reduces \(\mathrm{IsProper}\,\pi\) to four ingredients --- quasi-compactness,
2133	quasi-separatedness, local finiteness of type, and the valuative criterion
2134	\(\mathrm{Existence} \sqcap \mathrm{Uniqueness}\). The first three and the
2135	uniqueness half are cheap (uniqueness is free from separatedness), so the only
2136	genuine content is \(\mathrm{Existence}\): every commutative square from a
2137	valuation ring admits a diagonal filler. This existence statement is the Nitsure
2138	chart-selection argument, which we decompose into four steps E1--E4.
2139	
2140	\subsection*{Cheap ingredients and the reduction to existence}

2142	\begin{lemma}

2144	  \label{lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme}

2146	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_affine_chart, def:gr_glued_scheme}
2147	  The underlying topological space of the glued Grassmannian scheme
2148	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) (\cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}) is quasi-compact: it carries the
2149	  structure of a \texttt{CompactSpace}.
2150	\end{lemma}
2151	\begin{proof}
2152	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_affine_chart}
2153	  The glue datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) is indexed by the finite set
2154	  \(\{\, I \subseteq \{1,\dots,r\} : \#(I) = d \,\}\) of size-\(d\) subsets, and
2155	  each chart \(U^I = \Spec \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) (\cref{def:gr_affine_chart}) is the
2156	  \(\Spec\) of a ring, hence quasi-compact. A scheme glued from finitely many
2157	  quasi-compact charts is quasi-compact, so \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a compact
2158	  space.

2185	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ}
2186	  Being locally of finite type is local on the source, so it may be checked on the
2187	  chart open cover (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}). On the chart \(U^I\) the composite
2188	  \(U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \xrightarrow{\pi} \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is, by
2189	  \cref{lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ}, the \(\Spec\) of the structure map \(\mathbb{Z} \to R^I
2190	  = \mathbb{Z}[X^I]\). The polynomial ring \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) is a finitely
2191	  generated \(\mathbb{Z}\)-algebra, so this map is of finite type; hence \(\pi\) is
2192	  locally of finite type.
2193	\end{proof}

2198	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.quasiSeparated_toSpecZ}
2199	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2200	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2201	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) is quasi-separated.
2202	\end{lemma}

2206	  separated morphism is quasi-separated.

2209	\begin{lemma}
2210	[Uniqueness half of the valuative criterion]
2211	  \label{lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}
2212	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}
2213	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2214	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2215	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) satisfies the \emph{uniqueness} part of the valuative
2216	  criterion: for a valuation ring \(R\) with fraction field \(K\), any two
2217	  morphisms \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\) that agree
2218	  on the generic point \(\Spec K\) coincide.
2219	\end{lemma}
2220	\begin{proof}
2221	  \uses{lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}
2222	  The structure morphism is separated (\cref{lem:gr_separated_toSpecZ}), and the
2223	  uniqueness half of the valuative criterion is free for any separated morphism:
2224	  two lifts agreeing on the schematically dense generic point of \(\Spec R\)
2225	  coincide because the diagonal is a (closed, in particular separated) immersion.
2226	\end{proof}

2228	\begin{lemma}
2229	[Properness from the existence half of the valuative criterion]
2230	  \label{lem:gr_isProper_of_valuativeExistence}
2231	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.isProper_of_valuativeExistence}
2232	  \uses{lem:gr_compactSpace_scheme, lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ,
2233	    lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ, lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ,
2234	    lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ, def:gr_to_specZ}
2235	  Suppose the structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2236	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) satisfies the \emph{existence} part of the valuative
2237	  criterion. Then \(\pi\) is proper.
2238	\end{lemma}
2239	\begin{proof}
2240	  \uses{lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ, lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ,
2241	    lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ, lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}
2242	  The Mathlib criterion \texttt{IsProper.of\_valuativeCriterion} reduces properness
2243	  of \(\pi\) to four hypotheses: \(\pi\) is quasi-compact
2244	  (\cref{lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ}), quasi-separated
2245	  (\cref{lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ}), locally of finite type
2246	  (\cref{lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ}), and satisfies the full valuative
2247	  criterion. The full valuative criterion is the conjunction of its existence and
2248	  uniqueness halves; the uniqueness half holds by
2249	  \cref{lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}, and the existence half is the
2250	  hypothesis. Assembling the four ingredients gives properness.
2251	\end{proof}

2253	\subsection*{The existence obligation: chart selection by minimal valuation}
2254	
2255	The existence half of the valuative criterion is the genuine geometric content,
2256	and it is exactly Nitsure's chart-selection argument. A commutative square over a
2257	valuation ring presents a valuation ring \(R\) with fraction field \(K =
2258	\operatorname{Frac}(R)\), valuation \(v := \mathrm{val}_R : K \to \Gamma\) (written
2259	multiplicatively, so \(R = \{x : v(x) \le 1\}\)), a morphism \(i_1 : \Spec K \to
2260	\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\), and a morphism \(i_2 : \Spec R \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) with
2261	\(i_1 \circ \pi = \Spec(R \to K) \circ i_2\); one must construct a filler
2262	\(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) making both triangles commute. We carry this out
2263	in four steps. The algebraic core --- the behaviour of the transition hom on a
2264	\(K'\)-minor --- is the following identity over the localised chart ring.
2265	
2266	\begin{lemma}
2267	[The minor-ratio identity]
2268	  \label{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul}
2269	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul}
2270	  \uses{def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det, lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet,
2271	    lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col, lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities,
2272	    lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul}
2273	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
2274	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L878-L883).
2275	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2276	  % "Now consider the homomorphism
2277	  %  $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$ defined by entries of the matrix formula
2278	  %  $$g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1}\,X^I)$$
2279	  %  Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
2280	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
2281	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$."
2282	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2283	  For size-\(d\) subsets \(I, J, K\), the pre-localisation transition hom
2284	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}\) (\cref{def:gr_transition_pre}) satisfies, in \(R^I_J =
2285	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\), the minor-ratio identity
2286	  \[
2287	    \tilde\theta_{I,J}\bigl(P^J_K\bigr)\cdot P^I_J \;=\; P^I_K
2288	  \]
2289	  (where \(P^I_J, P^I_K\) are read through the structure map \(\mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to
2290	  R^I_J\)). Equivalently \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) = P^I_K / P^I_J\), the
2291	  determinant of the \(K\)-minor of \((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). This generalises
2292	  \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_swap_mul} (the case \(K = I\), where
2293	  \(P^I_I = 1\)) and is the algebraic engine of the existence argument: pulled back
2294	  through a \(K\)-point \(f\) it gives \(g(P^J_K) = f(P^I_K)/f(P^I_J)\).
2295	\end{lemma}
2296	\begin{proof}
2297	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col,
2298	    lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}
2299	  By \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}, \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K) =
2300	  \det(M_K)\) is the determinant of the \(K\)-minor of the image matrix \(M =
2301	  (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\). Splitting the \(K\)-column submatrix of the product
2302	  (\cref{lem:gr_mul_submatrix_col}) and using multiplicativity of the determinant,
2303	  \(\det(M_K) = \det\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1}\bigr)\cdot \det(X^I_K) =
2304	  \det(X^I_J)^{-1}\cdot P^I_K\). Multiplying by \(P^I_J = \det(X^I_J)\) and
2305	  cancelling \(\det(X^I_J)^{-1}\det(X^I_J) = 1\)
2306	  (\cref{lem:gr_universalMinorInv_identities}) gives
2307	  \(\tilde\theta_{I,J}(P^J_K)\cdot P^I_J = P^I_K\).
2308	\end{proof}
2309	
2310	\begin{lemma}
2311	[E1 --- the \(K\)-point factors through a single chart]
2312	  \label{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization}
2313	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.existence_chart_factorization}
2314	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_glued_scheme, def:gr_affine_chart,
2315	    lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}
2316	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
2317	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L868-L870).
2318	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2319	  % "Let $\Rr$ be a dvr, $\K$ its quotient field, and let
2320	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$ be a morphism. This is given by
2321	  %  a ring homomorphism $f : \Z[X^I] \to \K$ for some $I$."
2322	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). From the valuative square data,
2323	  %   produce `I : {I : Finset (Fin r) // I.card = d}` together with a ring hom
2324	  %   `f : R^I = MvPolynomial (Fin d × {q // q ∉ I.1}) ℤ → K` and a factorization
2325	  %   `i₁ = Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom f) ≫ (theGlueData d r).ι I`.
2326	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2327	  The morphism \(i_1 : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) factors through a single
2328	  chart: there exist a size-\(d\) subset \(I\) and a ring homomorphism \(f : R^I =
2329	  \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\) such that
2330	  \[
2331	    i_1 \;=\; \Spec(f) \circ \iota_I : \Spec K \to U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d),
2332	  \]
2333	  where \(\iota_I : U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is the chart immersion of the glue
2334	  datum (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}).
2335	\end{lemma}
2336	\begin{proof}
2337	  \uses{def:gr_the_glue_data, def:gr_glued_scheme, lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion}
2338	  Since \(K\) is a field, \(\Spec K\) is a one-point space, so its image point \(x
2339	  := i_1(\ast) \in \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) is a single point. The chart immersions
2340	  \(\{\iota_I : U^I \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\}\) of the glue datum
2341	  (\cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) are jointly surjective (their images cover the
2342	  glued scheme \cref{def:gr_glued_scheme}), so \(x\) lies in the range of some
2343	  \(\iota_I\). Each \(\iota_I\) is an open immersion
2344	  (\cref{lem:gr_chartIncl_isOpenImmersion} gives this for the chart inclusions, and
2345	  the glue datum's immersions inherit it). A morphism out of \(\Spec K\) whose
2346	  image point lies in the range of an open immersion factors uniquely through that
2347	  open immersion; hence \(i_1 = j \circ \iota_I\) for a unique \(j : \Spec K \to
2348	  U^I\). As \(U^I = \Spec R^I\) is affine, \(j = \Spec(f)\) for a unique ring map
2349	  \(f : R^I \to K\), giving the claimed factorization.
2350	\end{proof}
2351	
2352	\begin{lemma}
2353	[E2 --- minimal-valuation chart selection]
2354	  \label{lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation}
2355	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.existence_minimal_valuation}
2356	  \uses{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization, def:gr_minor_det,
2357	    lem:gr_minorDet_self}
2358	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
2359	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L870-L876).
2360	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2361	  % "Having fixed
2362	  %  one such $I$, next choose $J$ such that $\nu(f(P^I_J))$ is minimum,
2363	  %  where $\nu : \K \to \Z\bigcup \{ \infty\}$ denotes
2364	  %  the discrete valuation. As $P^I_I = 1$, note that
2365	  %  $\nu(f(P^I_J)) \le 0$, therefore
2366	  %  $f(P^I_J) \ne 0$ in $\K$ and so the matrix $f(X^I_J)$
2367	  %  lies in $GL_d(\K)$."
2368	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). With `v := ValuationRing.valuation
2369	  %   R K`, choose `J : {J : Finset (Fin r) // J.card = d}` maximizing
2370	  %   `v (f (minorDet d r I J))` over the finite index set, and conclude
2371	  %   `f (minorDet d r I J) ≠ 0` (equivalently `v (f (minorDet d r I J)) ≥ 1`).
2372	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2373	  Fix \(I\) and \(f : R^I \to K\) as in \cref{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization},
2374	  and let \(v := \mathrm{val}_R : K \to \Gamma\) be the (multiplicative) valuation
2375	  of \(R\). Over the finite index set \(\{\, J : \#(J) = d \,\}\) choose \(J\)
2376	  \emph{maximising} \(v\bigl(f(P^I_J)\bigr)\) (Nitsure's minimal additive valuation
2377	  \(\nu\) is the maximal multiplicative \(v\)). Then \(I\) itself is a candidate and
2378	  \(P^I_I = 1\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_self}) gives \(v(f(P^I_I)) = v(1) = 1\), so
2379	  the maximum satisfies \(v(f(P^I_J)) \ge 1 > 0\). In particular \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\),
2380	  so the matrix \(f(X^I_J)\) is invertible over \(K\).
2381	\end{lemma}
2382	\begin{proof}
2383	  \uses{lem:gr_minorDet_self, def:gr_minor_det}
2384	  The index set of size-\(d\) subsets is finite and nonempty (\(I\) is a member),
2385	  so the function \(J \mapsto v(f(P^I_J))\) attains a maximum at some \(J\). Since
2386	  \(P^I_I = 1\) (\cref{lem:gr_minorDet_self}) and \(v(1) = 1\), the value at \(I\)
2387	  is \(1\); hence the maximum is \(\ge 1\), so \(v(f(P^I_J)) \ne 0\) in \(\Gamma\),
2388	  i.e.\ \(f(P^I_J) \ne 0\). A square matrix over the field \(K\) with nonzero
2389	  determinant \(f(P^I_J) = \det f(X^I_J)\) is invertible.
2390	\end{proof}

2392	\begin{lemma}
2393	[E3 --- entries land in \(R\); \(g\) factors through the valuation ring]
2394	  \label{lem:gr_existence_factor_through_valuation_ring}
2395	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.existence_factor_through_valuationRing}
2396	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul, lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation,
2397	    lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross,
2398	    lem:mathlib_away_lift, def:gr_transition_pre, def:gr_minor_det}
2399	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Properness" (read from
2400	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L881-L887).
2401	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2402	  % "Then $g$ defines the same morphism $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$,
2403	  %  and moreover all $d\times d$ minors $X^J_K$ satisfy
2404	  %  $\nu(g(P^J_K)) \ge 0$.
2405	  %  As the minor $X^J_J$ is identity, it follows from the above that in fact
2406	  %  $\nu(g(x^J_{p,q}))\ge 0$ for all entries of $X^J$.
2407	  %  Therefore, the map $g : \Z[X^J] \to \K$
2408	  %  factors uniquely via $\Rr \subset \K$."
2409	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). Set
2410	  %   `g := f' ∘ transitionPreMap d r I J`, where `f' : R^I_J → K` is the
2411	  %   `IsLocalization.Away.lift` of `f` along `minorDet d r I J` (a unit by E2).
2412	  %   Prove `∀ x, g x ∈ (algebraMap R K).range`, hence factor `g = (algebraMap R K)
2413	  %   ∘ g'` for a unique ring hom `g' : R^J → R`.
2414	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2415	  Define \(g : R^J = \mathbb{Z}[X^J] \to K\) by the matrix formula \(g(X^J) =
2416	  f\bigl((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\bigr)\); concretely \(g = f' \circ \tilde\theta_{I,J}\),
2417	  where \(f' : R^I_J \to K\) is the extension of \(f\) to the away-localisation
2418	  \(R^I_J = \mathbb{Z}[X^I, 1/P^I_J]\) (well-defined by the universal property
2419	  \cref{lem:mathlib_away_lift}, since \(f(P^I_J)\) is a unit of \(K\) by
2420	  \cref{lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation}). Then every value \(g(P^J_K)\) lies in
2421	  \(R\), and consequently \(g\) factors uniquely as \(g = (R \hookrightarrow K)
2422	  \circ g'\) for a ring homomorphism \(g' : R^J \to R\). Moreover \(g\) defines the
2423	  same \(K\)-point as \(f\).
2424	\end{lemma}
2425	\begin{proof}
2426	  \uses{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul, lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation,
2427	    lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet, lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}
2428	  Applying \(f'\) to the minor-ratio identity
2429	  (\cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet_mul}), and using that \(f'\) extends
2430	  \(f\), gives for every size-\(d\) subset \(K'\)
2431	  \[
2432	    g\bigl(P^J_{K'}\bigr)\cdot f(P^I_J) \;=\; f(P^I_{K'}),
2433	    \qquad\text{so}\qquad
2434	    g\bigl(P^J_{K'}\bigr) \;=\; \frac{f(P^I_{K'})}{f(P^I_J)} .
2435	  \]
2436	  By the maximality in \cref{lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation},
2437	  \(v(f(P^I_{K'})) \le v(f(P^I_J))\), hence \(v\bigl(g(P^J_{K'})\bigr) =
2438	  v(f(P^I_{K'}))/v(f(P^I_J)) \le 1\), i.e.\ \(g(P^J_{K'}) \in R\). (That
2439	  \(f(P^I_J)\) is a unit and the cross minors map to units is recorded by
2440	  \cref{lem:gr_isUnit_incl_transitionPreMap_cross}, via the determinant identity of
2441	  \cref{lem:gr_transitionPreMap_minorDet}.) Now the \(J\)-minor \(X^J_J\) of \(X^J\)
2442	  is the identity, so cofactor expansion expresses each free entry \(x^J_{p,q}\)
2443	  (\(q \notin J\)) as, up to sign, the minor \(P^J_{K'}\) with \(K' = (J \setminus
2444	  \{j_p\}) \cup \{q\}\) --- the determinant obtained by replacing the \(p\)-th
2445	  identity column of \(X^J_J\) by column \(q\). Hence \(g(x^J_{p,q}) = \pm
2446	  g(P^J_{K'}) \in R\), while the entries with \(q \in J\) are \(0\) or \(1\),
2447	  already in \(R\). (This entry-as-minor sign bookkeeping is the one sub-step with
2448	  no pre-existing matrix-algebra scaffold; it requires expanding the determinant of
2449	  a column-substituted identity matrix.) Since \(g\) sends every generator of
2450	  \(\mathbb{Z}[X^J]\) into \(R\), it factors uniquely through the subring \(R
2451	  \subseteq K\) as \(g = (R \hookrightarrow K) \circ g'\) with \(g' : R^J \to R\).
2452	\end{proof}

2454	\begin{lemma}
2455	[E4 --- the filler and its two triangles]
2456	  \label{lem:gr_existence_lift}
2457	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.existence_lift}
2458	  \uses{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization,
2459	    lem:gr_existence_factor_through_valuation_ring, def:gr_the_glue_data,
2460	    def:gr_to_specZ, lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ}

2463	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2464	  % "The resulting morphism of
2465	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs
2466	  %  $\varphi : \spec \K \to \grass(r,d)$."
2467	  % LEAN SIGNATURE (intended scaffold target). The filler is
2468	  %   `Spec.map (CommRingCat.ofHom g') ≫ (theGlueData d r).ι J`; assemble a
2469	  %   `CommSq.LiftStruct` (top triangle from E1 + `g = algebraMap ∘ g'` + ι_toSpecZ,
2470	  %   bottom triangle from `specZIsTerminal.hom_ext`) and conclude `HasLift`.
2471	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1.}
2472	  The morphism
2473	  \[
2474	    \ell \;:=\; \Spec(g') \circ \iota_J : \Spec R \to U^J \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)
2475	  \]
2476	  (with \(g' : R^J \to R\) from
2477	  \cref{lem:gr_existence_factor_through_valuation_ring} and \(\iota_J\) the chart
2478	  immersion of \cref{def:gr_the_glue_data}) is a diagonal filler of the valuative
2479	  square: it restricts to \(i_1\) over the generic point \(\Spec K\), and it lies
2480	  over \(\Spec \mathbb{Z}\). Hence the valuative square has a lift.

2482	\begin{proof}
2483	  \uses{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization,
2484	    lem:gr_existence_factor_through_valuation_ring, lem:gr_ι_toSpecZ,
2485	    def:gr_to_specZ}
2486	  \emph{Top triangle.} Restricting \(\ell\) along \(\Spec(R \to K)\) yields
2487	  \(\Spec(g' \text{ followed by } R \hookrightarrow K) \circ \iota_J = \Spec(g)
2488	  \circ \iota_J\), using \(g = (R \hookrightarrow K)\circ g'\)
2489	  (\cref{lem:gr_existence_factor_through_valuation_ring}). Since \(g = f' \circ
2490	  \tilde\theta_{I,J}\) presents the same \(K\)-point as \(f\), and \(i_1 = \Spec(f)
2491	  \circ \iota_I\) (\cref{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization}), this composite
2492	  equals \(i_1\). \emph{Bottom triangle.} Both \(\ell \circ \pi\) and the given
2493	  \(i_2\) are morphisms \(\Spec R \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) into the terminal object

2500	\begin{lemma}
2501	[Existence half of the valuative criterion]
2502	  \label{lem:gr_valuativeExistence_toSpecZ}
2503	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Grassmannian.valuativeExistence_toSpecZ}
2504	  \uses{def:gr_to_specZ, lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization,

2509	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2510	  % "We now show that $\pi : \grass(r,d) \to \spec \Z$ is proper. It is enough to
2511	  %  verify the valuative criterion of properness for discrete valuation rings.
2512	  %  ... The resulting morphism of
2513	  %  schemes $\spec \Rr \to U^J \hra \grass(r,d)$ prolongs

2516	  The structure morphism \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\)
2517	  (\cref{def:gr_to_specZ}) satisfies the \emph{existence} part of the valuative
2518	  criterion: every commutative square from a valuation ring \(R\) (with fraction
2519	  field \(K\)) admits a diagonal filler \(\Spec R \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\).
2520	\end{lemma}

2522	  \uses{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization, lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation,
2523	    lem:gr_existence_factor_through_valuation_ring, lem:gr_existence_lift}
2524	  Given a valuative square, factor the generic-point morphism through a chart
2525	  (\cref{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization}), select \(J\) by maximal valuation
2526	  (\cref{lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation}), factor the transported ring map
2527	  \(g\) through the valuation ring (\cref{lem:gr_existence_factor_through_valuation_ring}),
2528	  and take the filler \(\Spec(g') \circ \iota_J\)
2529	  (\cref{lem:gr_existence_lift}). This produces the required diagonal filler, so the
2530	  existence half holds.

2595	\begin{proof}
2596	  \uses{lem:gr_isProper_of_valuativeExistence, lem:gr_valuativeExistence_toSpecZ}
2597	  Nitsure's argument is precisely the valuative criterion, organised here so that
2598	  all the topological/finiteness bookkeeping is separated from the one geometric
2599	  step. The reduction \cref{lem:gr_isProper_of_valuativeExistence} packages
2600	  quasi-compactness (\cref{lem:gr_quasiCompact_toSpecZ}), quasi-separatedness
2601	  (\cref{lem:gr_quasiSeparated_toSpecZ}), local finiteness of type
2602	  (\cref{lem:gr_locallyOfFiniteType_toSpecZ}) and the uniqueness half
2603	  (\cref{lem:gr_valuativeUniqueness_toSpecZ}, free from separatedness) into the
2604	  hypothesis that \(\pi\) satisfies only the \emph{existence} half of the valuative
2605	  criterion. That existence half is established in
2606	  \cref{lem:gr_valuativeExistence_toSpecZ} by the chart-selection argument of steps
2607	  E1--E4: the generic-point morphism lands in a chart \(U^I\) via a ring map \(f\)
2608	  (\cref{lem:gr_existence_chart_factorization}); one selects \(J\) with
2609	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) minimal --- equivalently \(v(f(P^I_J))\) maximal ---
2610	  (\cref{lem:gr_existence_minimal_valuation}), so \(f(X^I_J) \in \mathrm{GL}_d(K)\);
2611	  the transported map \(g(X^J) = f((X^I_J)^{-1} X^I)\) then sends every generator
2612	  into the valuation ring and factors as \(g = (R \hookrightarrow K)\circ g'\)
2613	  (\cref{lem:gr_existence_factor_through_valuation_ring}); and \(\Spec(g') \circ
2614	  \iota_J\) is the prolonging filler (\cref{lem:gr_existence_lift}). Feeding this
2615	  existence statement into \cref{lem:gr_isProper_of_valuativeExistence} yields that
2616	  \(\pi : \mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) is proper.
2617	\end{proof}

2619	\section{Out of scope}
2620	\label{sec:gr_out_of_scope}
2621	
2622	This chapter constructs the Grassmannian \emph{scheme} \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over
2623	the absolute base \(\mathbb{Z}\) and establishes its smoothness, separatedness
2624	and properness. The following constructions are deferred to later chapters and
2625	are not treated here:
2626	\begin{itemize}
2627	  \item \textbf{The tautological/universal quotient} \(u : \mathcal{O}^{\oplus
2628	    r}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) and the determinant
2629	    line bundle \(\det \mathcal{U}\) with its Pl\"ucker embedding.
2630	  \item \textbf{The functor-of-points representability} of \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\)
2631	    -- that it represents \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d) =
2632	    \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}}}_{\oplus^r \mathcal{O}_{\mathbb{Z}} /
2633	    \mathbb{Z} / \mathbb{Z}}\); this is blueprinted as
2634	    \cref{thm:grassmannian_representable} in
2635	    \cref{chap:Picard_QuotScheme}.
2636	  \item \textbf{The relative version over a general base \(S\)} with a locally
2637	    free sheaf \(V\) of rank \(r\): the scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V,d)\) obtained by
2638	    base change and gluing over a trivialising cover of \(V\). The
2639	    absolute-over-\(\mathbb{Z}\) construction of this chapter is the input to
2640	    that base change.
2641	\end{itemize}
