1865	\subsubsection*{Ambient finiteness transfer}
1866	
1867	\begin{lemma}\leanok
1868	[Abstract single-pair finiteness transfer down one variable]
1869	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}
1870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_finite_transfer}
1871	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1872	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1873	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib,
1874	    lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}
1875	  Let \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) be pairwise-commuting \(\kappa\)-linear endomorphisms of
1876	  \(M\), and let \(P' \le P\) be \(\kappa\)-submodules of \(M\), each stable under
1877	  every \(t_i\). Suppose the last endomorphism \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\)
1878	  (\(x \cdot P \subseteq P'\)), and that the subquotient \(P/P'\) is finite over the
1879	  free polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\) for the polynomial-module structure
1880	  of \cref{def:graded_polyModule} (with carriers the ambient \(t\)-stable submodules
1881	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\), \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\),
1882	  \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). Then \(P/P'\) is finite over
1883	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\).
1884	
1885	  This is the abstract single-pair transfer: it does not mention the kernel/cokernel
1886	  pairs, but is the engine applied to each of them by the kernel and cokernel
1887	  constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
1888	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}). Because the last variable
1889	  annihilates the subquotient, the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-action factors through
1890	  the last-variable surjection \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r] \twoheadrightarrow
1891	  \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), which is what makes the transfer drop one variable.
1892	\end{lemma}
1893	
1894	\begin{proof}
1895	  \uses{def:graded_polyModule, def:graded_polySubmodule,
1896	    lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom, lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem,
1897	    lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib, lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}
1898	    \leanok
1899	  Write \(\sigma = \mathrm{lastVarAlgHom} : \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]
1900	  \twoheadrightarrow \kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\) for the surjective ring
1901	  homomorphism sending the last variable to \(0\) and each earlier variable \(X_i\)
1902	  (\(i < r\)) to \(X_i\) (\cref{lem:graded_lastVarAlgHom}). Consider the map on numerators
1903	  \[
1904	    g : \mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P \;\longrightarrow\;
1905	      \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/
1906	        \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr), \qquad y \longmapsto [\,y\,],
1907	  \]
1908	  where the source carries the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-module structure and the
1909	  target the reduced \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-module structure (both with the
1910	  same ambient carrier \(P\), \cref{def:graded_polySubmodule}). The mod-\(P'\)
1911	  semilinearity identity (\cref{lem:graded_polyEndHom_lastVar_sub_mem}) -- acting by
1912	  \(s\) through the full family and acting by \(\sigma(s)\) through the reduced family
1913	  agree modulo \(P'\), precisely because \(x = t_r\) carries \(P\) into \(P'\) --
1914	  says exactly that \(g\) is \(\sigma\)-semilinear. The map \(g\) is surjective (it is
1915	  the quotient projection on the common carrier), and it kills the denominator
1916	  \(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\), so it descends through that submodule
1917	  (\cref{lem:submodule_liftQ_mathlib}) to a surjective
1918	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\)-linear map
1919	  \[
1920	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1921	      \;\twoheadrightarrow\;
1922	    \bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P\bigr)\big/\bigl(\mathrm{polySubmodule}\,P'\bigr)
1923	  \]
1924	  from the \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finite subquotient onto the reduced one.
1925	  Finiteness transfers along this surjection
1926	  (\cref{lem:module_finite_of_surjective_mathlib}), giving finiteness of \(P/P'\)
1927	  over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\).
1928	
1929	  \emph{Why the free polynomial ring.} The smaller ring must be the \emph{free}
1930	  polynomial ring \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}]\), not the subalgebra
1931	  \(\mathrm{adjoin}_\kappa\{t_0, \dots, t_{r-1}\} \subseteq
1932	  \operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\): relations among the endomorphisms inside
1933	  \(\operatorname{End}_\kappa(M)\) would obstruct the polynomial-ring surjection
1934	  \(\sigma\) that the scalar transfer requires
1935	  (\cref{lem:fg_restrictScalars_of_surjective_mathlib}). Keeping free polynomial
1936	  rings at every step keeps \(\sigma\) -- and hence the transfer -- available
1937	  throughout. Everything is finite generation of ambient submodules of the fixed
1938	  \(M\) and their quotients.
1939	\end{proof}

1941	\subsubsection*{Subquotient constructors}
1942	
1943	The kernel/cokernel closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) and the
1944	abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}) are
1945	bundled into two constructors that take a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum to the
1946	length-\(r\) kernel and cokernel data consumed by the induction. The two stability
1947	lemmas below first record that the new pairs are stable under the \emph{whole}
1948	endomorphism family (not merely the reduced one), which is what lets the over-\(\kappa[t_0,\dots,t_r]\)
1949	finiteness inputs be stated before the variable is dropped.
1950	
1951	\begin{lemma}
1952	
1953	\leanok
1954	[The kernel pair's lower module is stable under the full family]
1955	  \label{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}
1956	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.ker_stable_full}
1957	  \uses{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}
1958	  Let \((N, N')\) be a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum
1959	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with endomorphisms \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and
1960	  \(x = t_r\). Then the kernel pair's lower module \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) is stable
1961	  under \emph{every} \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)), not only the reduced family: each
1962	  \(t_i\) preserves \(N\) and preserves the preimage \(x^{-1}(N')\) because it
1963	  commutes with \(x\) and preserves \(N'\)
1964	  (\cref{lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute}). This full stability is what allows the
1965	  kernel subquotient's finiteness to be measured over \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)
1966	  before the transfer drops the last variable.
1967	\end{lemma}
1968	
1969	\begin{lemma}
1970	
1971	\leanok
1972	[The cokernel pair's upper module is stable under the full family]
1973	  \label{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}
1974	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.coker_stable_full}
1975	  \uses{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}
1976	  Under the same hypotheses, the cokernel pair's upper module \(N' + x \cdot N\) is
1977	  stable under every \(t_i\) (\(0 \le i \le r\)): each \(t_i\) preserves \(N'\) and
1978	  preserves the image \(x \cdot N\) because it commutes with \(x\) and preserves
1979	  \(N\) (\cref{lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute}), and a map preserves a sum of
1980	  preserved submodules. This full stability supports the over-\(\kappa[t_0, \dots,
1981	  t_r]\) finiteness input of the cokernel constructor.
1982	\end{lemma}
1983	
1984	\begin{definition}
1985	
1986	\leanok
1987	[Kernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
1988	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker}
1989	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.ker}
1990	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,

1992	    lem:graded_comap_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_ker_stable_full,
1993	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
1994	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}
1995	  From a length-\((r+1)\) subquotient datum \((N, N')\) with endomorphisms
1996	  \(t_0, \dots, t_r\) and \(x = t_r\) (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}), the
1997	  \emph{kernel constructor} produces the length-\(r\) datum
1998	  \[
1999	    \ker D \;=\; \bigl(\,N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\ N'\,\bigr)
2000	  \]
2001	  on the reduced family \((t_0, \dots, t_{r-1})\). Its
2002	  non-finiteness fields are supplied by the ambient kernel calculus: homogeneity of
2003	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_isHomogeneous}), the nesting
2004	  \(N' \le N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_ker_le}), and stability under the
2005	  reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_ker_stable_full}). The finiteness witness
2006	  is obtained by first shrinking the numerator from \(N\) to
2007	  \(N \cap x^{-1}(N')\) (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_numerator}) to get
2008	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the kernel subquotient, then transferring
2009	  down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}), valid because
2010	  \(x\) annihilates the kernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_ker_annihilate}). This
2011	  realises the kernel half of \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine
2012	  length-\(r\) datum.
2013	\end{definition}
2014	
2015	\begin{definition}
2016	
2017	\leanok
2018	[Cokernel constructor of a subquotient datum]
2019	  \label{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}
2020	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.SubquotientDatum.coker}
2021	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2022	    lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous, lem:graded_coker_le, lem:graded_coker_annihilate,
2023	    lem:graded_map_map_le_of_commute, lem:graded_coker_stable_full,
2024	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer,
2025	    lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}
2026	  From the same length-\((r+1)\) datum, the \emph{cokernel constructor} produces the
2027	  length-\(r\) datum
2028	  \[
2029	    \operatorname{coker} D \;=\; \bigl(\,N,\ N' + x \cdot N\,\bigr)
2030	  \]
2031	  on the reduced family. Its non-finiteness fields come from the ambient cokernel
2032	  calculus: homogeneity of \(N' + x \cdot N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_isHomogeneous}),
2033	  the nesting \(N' + x \cdot N \le N\) (\cref{lem:graded_coker_le}), and stability
2034	  under the reduced family (\cref{lem:graded_coker_stable_full}). The finiteness field
2035	  is obtained by enlarging the denominator from \(N'\) to \(N' + x \cdot N\)
2036	  (\cref{lem:graded_polyQuot_finite_of_le_denominator}) to get
2037	  \(\kappa[t_0, \dots, t_r]\)-finiteness of the cokernel subquotient, then
2038	  transferring down one variable (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}),
2039	  valid because \(x\) annihilates the cokernel subquotient
2040	  (\cref{lem:graded_coker_annihilate}). This realises the cokernel half of
2041	  \cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker} as a genuine length-\(r\) datum.
2042	\end{definition}

2044	\subsubsection*{The degreewise difference identity}
2045	
2046	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2047	[$D_5$ -- degreewise rank--nullity difference]
2048	  \label{lem:graded_degreewise_finrank_diff}
2049	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.degreewise_finrank_diff}
2050	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2051	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2052	  Let \(\varphi : V \to W\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map between finite-dimensional
2053	  \(\kappa\)-vector spaces. Then
2054	  \[
2055	    \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V
2056	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) - \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi).
2057	  \]
2058	  Applied degreewise with \(\varphi = (x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\),
2059	  \(V = \mathcal{M}_n\), \(W = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}\) -- so that \(\ker\varphi\) is the
2060	  degree-\(n\) piece \(K_n\) of the kernel subquotient and
2061	  \(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi = \mathcal{M}_{n+1}/x \mathcal{M}_n\) the
2062	  degree-\((n+1)\) piece \(C_{n+1}\) of the cokernel subquotient
2063	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}, via the split \(G_1\)
2064	  \cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSup_eq} and the degree shift
2065	  \cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}) -- this gives, for every \(n\),
2066	  \[
2067	    \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa \mathcal{M}_n
2068	      \;=\; \dim_\kappa C_{n+1} - \dim_\kappa K_n,
2069	  \]
2070	  which is the difference identity \(\mathrm{hdiff}\) consumed by
2071	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}. No graded structure is used in proving the
2072	  underlying vector-space identity -- it is pure rank--nullity.
2073	\end{lemma}
2074	
2075	\begin{proof}
2076	  \uses{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib,
2077	    lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}
2078	    \leanok
2079	  By rank--nullity (\cref{lem:finrank_range_add_finrank_ker_mathlib}),
2080	  \(\dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi) + \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}
2081	  \varphi)\), so \(\dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa V -
2082	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). By additivity on the short exact sequence
2083	  \(0 \to \operatorname{range}\varphi \to W \to W/\operatorname{range}\varphi \to 0\)
2084	  (\cref{lem:finrank_ses_additive_mathlib}),
2085	  \(\dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W -
2086	  \dim_\kappa(\operatorname{range}\varphi) = \dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V +
2087	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). Rearranging gives
2088	  \(\dim_\kappa W - \dim_\kappa V = \dim_\kappa(W/\operatorname{range}\varphi) -
2089	  \dim_\kappa(\ker\varphi)\). The degreewise application identifies
2090	  \(\ker(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\) and
2091	  \(\mathcal{M}_{n+1}/\operatorname{range}(x : \mathcal{M}_n \to \mathcal{M}_{n+1})\)
2092	  with the degree-\(n\) piece of the kernel subquotient and the degree-\((n+1)\)
2093	  piece of the cokernel subquotient (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2094	  the latter because the degree-\((n+1)\) graded piece of the homogeneous submodule
2095	  \(x\mathcal{M}\) is exactly \(x \mathcal{M}_n\)
2096	  (\cref{lem:isHomogeneousElem_graded_smul_mathlib}).
2097	\end{proof}

2099	\subsubsection*{The base case ($r = 0$)}
2100	
2101	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2102	[Base-case finiteness over the empty polynomial ring]
2103	  \label{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2104	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2105	  Let \(Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-vector space that is also a module over
2106	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\) for an \emph{empty} index set
2107	  \(\sigma\) -- in particular \(\sigma = \{0, \dots, r-1\}\) with \(r = 0\) -- in a way
2108	  compatible with the \(\kappa\)-action (scalar tower). If \(Q\) is module-finite over
2109	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa)\), then \(Q\) is finite-dimensional over
2110	  \(\kappa\). Indeed for empty \(\sigma\) the polynomial ring is
2111	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\sigma, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), a finite \(\kappa\)-module,
2112	  so finiteness of \(Q\) over it transfers to finiteness over \(\kappa\) along the scalar
2113	  tower. This is the length-zero input to the ambient subquotient induction
2114	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}): when \(r = 0\) the subquotient \(N/N'\) is a
2115	  finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space, hence \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero.
2116	\end{lemma}
2117	
2118	\begin{lemma}
2119	
2120	\leanok
2121	[independence of images modulo the kernel]
2122	  \label{lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2123	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup}
2124	  Let \(\pi : A \to Q\) be a \(\kappa\)-linear map and \((g_i)_{i \in \iota}\) a family of
2125	  subspaces of \(A\) that is \emph{independent modulo \(\ker\pi\)}: for each \(i\), every
2126	  \(a \in g_i\) that also lies in \(\ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\) already lies in
2127	  \(\ker\pi\). Then the image family \((\pi(g_i))_{i \in \iota}\) is independent
2128	  (\(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\)) in \(Q\). Ring-agnostic lattice core of the base-case
2129	  independence step below; applied to the quotient map \(N \twoheadrightarrow N/N'\).
2130	\end{lemma}
2131	\begin{proof}
2132	
2133	\leanok
2134	  By the disjointness criterion for independence it suffices to show, for each \(i\),
2135	  that \(\pi(g_i) \cap \sum_{j \ne i} \pi(g_j) = 0\). Take \(q\) in that intersection;
2136	  write \(q = \pi(a)\) with \(a \in g_i\), and (pushing the sum through \(\pi\), since
2137	  \(\pi(\sum_{j\ne i} g_j) = \sum_{j\ne i}\pi(g_j)\)) \(q = \pi(b)\) with
2138	  \(b \in \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). Then \(a - b \in \ker\pi\), so
2139	  \(a = (a-b) + b \in \ker\pi + \sum_{j \ne i} g_j\). By the hypothesis applied at \(i\),
2140	  \(a \in \ker\pi\), whence \(q = \pi(a) = 0\).
2141	\end{proof}
2142	
2143	\begin{lemma}
2144	
2145	\leanok
2146	[Base case: the length-zero subquotient Hilbert function vanishes eventually]
2147	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2148	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}
2149	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb,
2150	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2151	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep, lem:graded_iSupIndep_map_of_mem_ker_sup,
2152	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2153	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2154	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2155	  % 00K1), proof, base case (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex,
2156	  % L13908-L13910).
2157	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2158	  % "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2159	  %  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2160	  %  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds."
2161	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (base case of the induction).}
2162	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(0\)
2163	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}): a homogeneous pair \(N' \le N\) of the fixed
2164	  ambient graded module \(M = \bigoplus_n \mathcal{M}_n\) with \emph{no} endomorphisms,
2165	  whose finiteness witness says the subquotient \(Q_0 := N/N'\) is module-finite over
2166	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa)\). Then the ambient Hilbert
2167	  function vanishes eventually: there is \(K \in \mathbb{N}\) with
2168	  \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for all \(n > K\). Consequently
2169	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\) (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}),
2170	  which is the base case of the ambient subquotient induction
2171	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}).
2172	\end{lemma}
2173	
2174	\begin{proof}
2175	  \uses{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite,
2176	    lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep,
2177	    lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero}
2178	    \leanok
2179	  Since the index set of variables is empty,
2180	  \(\operatorname{MvPolynomial}(\varnothing, \kappa) \cong \kappa\), so the finiteness
2181	  witness makes the subquotient \(Q_0 = N/N'\) a \emph{finite-dimensional}
2182	  \(\kappa\)-vector space
2183	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}). For each
2184	  degree \(n\), composing the inclusion of the homogeneous piece
2185	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \hookrightarrow N\) with the quotient projection
2186	  \(N \twoheadrightarrow Q_0\) gives a \(\kappa\)-linear map
2187	  \(\psi_n : N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \to Q_0\), \(m \mapsto [m]\), whose image is the
2188	  class of the degree-\(n\) homogeneous piece inside the subquotient.
2189	
2190	  \emph{Step 1: independence of the images.} The family
2191	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\bigr)_{n}\) is \(\mathrm{iSupIndep}\) in
2192	  \(Q_0\). The reason is the ambient direct-sum grading of \(M\): the homogeneous
2193	  pieces \(\mathcal{M}_n\) form an independent family
2194	  (\cref{lem:graded_homogeneousSubmodule_iSupIndep}). An element of
2195	  \(\bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)\) is a finite sum of classes of
2196	  homogeneous elements of degrees \(j \ne n\); since \(N'\) is homogeneous, the
2197	  degree-\(n\) homogeneous component of any representative of such a class lies in
2198	  \(N'\), so the class has zero degree-\(n\) component in \(Q_0\). A nonzero class in
2199	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\), by contrast, is represented by a homogeneous
2200	  element of degree \(n\) not in \(N'\), so has nonzero degree-\(n\) component.
2201	  Comparing degree-\(n\) components forces the intersection
2202	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \cap \bigsqcup_{j \ne n} \operatorname{range}(\psi_j)
2203	  = 0\); independence follows.
2204	  % NOTE: ROUTE (b). Prove the iSupIndep by direct membership analysis in
2205	  %   ⨆_{j ≠ n} range(ψ j), reading off the degree-n homogeneous component
2206	  %   directly inside Q₀'s fixed κ-module structure.
2207	  %   No outgoing linear map out of Q₀ is constructed, so no scalar ring other than κ
2208	  %   is ever introduced.
2209	  % NOTE: ROUTE (a). Building a κ-linear detector
2210	  %   Φ : Q₀ → M/N', [m] ↦ [π_n m] (degree-n projection) via descent through the
2211	  %   subquotient fails: the descended map carries the scalar action of Q₀'s base
2212	  %   ring, but the target M/N' carries only a κ-module structure -- a scalar-ring
2213	  %   incompatibility. Route (b) avoids this by working entirely within Q₀.
2214	
2215	  \emph{Step 2: finiteness of the support.} Because \(Q_0\) is finite-dimensional and
2216	  the ranges \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n)\) are independent, only finitely many of
2217	  them are nonzero: the set \(\{\,n : \operatorname{range}(\psi_n) \ne 0\,\}\) is
2218	  finite (\cref{lem:finite_ne_bot_of_iSupIndep_mathlib}). In particular it is bounded
2219	  above, by some \(K \in \mathbb{N}\).
2220	
2221	  \emph{Step 3: eventual vanishing.} For \(n > K\) we have
2222	  \(\operatorname{range}(\psi_n) = 0\), i.e.\ every homogeneous element of
2223	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n\) has class \(0\) in \(Q_0 = N/N'\); equivalently
2224	  \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n \le N'\), hence \(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n = N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n\)
2225	  and
2226	  \[
2227	    \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2228	      - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n) \;=\; 0 .
2229	  \]
2230	  Thus \(\mathrm{hilb}\) is eventually zero, and
2231	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2232	\end{proof}

2234	\subsubsection*{The ambient subquotient induction}
2235	
2236	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2237	[Rationality of the ambient subquotient Hilbert function]
2238	  \label{lem:graded_subquotient_isRatHilb}
2239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.GradedModule.subquotient_hilbertSeries_rational}
2240	  \uses{def:graded_subquotientHilb, lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2241	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2242	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2243	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2244	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2245	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2246	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2247	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], "Commutative Algebra", \S "Noetherian graded
2248	  % rings", Proposition \texttt{proposition-graded-hilbert-polynomial} (Tag
2249	  % 00K1), proof (read from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2250	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1 (induction on the number of
2251	  degree-one generators).}
2252	  Let \((N, N')\) be a subquotient datum of length \(r\)
2253	  (\cref{def:graded_subquotientHilb}) with ambient Hilbert function
2254	  \(\mathrm{hilb}\). Then \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\)
2255	  (\cref{def:ratHilb}): the difference \(n \mapsto \dim_\kappa(N \cap \mathcal{M}_n)
2256	  - \dim_\kappa(N' \cap \mathcal{M}_n)\) is, for \(n \gg 0\), the coefficient
2257	  sequence of a rational series \(p \cdot (1 - X)^{-r}\) with \(p \in
2258	  \mathbb{Q}[X]\). The induction is on the length \(r\), and every object that
2259	  appears is an ambient pair of homogeneous submodules of the fixed graded module
2260	  \(M\).
2261	\end{lemma}
2262	
2263	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: [Stacks Project], Tag 00K1, proof, induction set-up (read
2264	% from references/hilbert-serre-algebra.tex, L13908-L13914).
2265	% "We prove this by induction on the minimal number of
2266	%  generators of $S_1$. If this number is $0$, then
2267	%  $M_n = 0$ for all $n \gg 0$ and the result holds.
2268	%  To prove the induction step, let $x\in S_1$
2269	%  be one of a minimal set of generators, such that
2270	%  the induction hypothesis applies to the
2271	%  graded ring $S/(x)$."
2272	\begin{proof}
2273	  \uses{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker,
2274	    def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker, def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker,
2275	    lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff,
2276	    lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer, lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero,
2277	    lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq, lem:ratHilb_bump,
2278	    lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero,
2279	    lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}
2280	    \leanok
2281	  We induct on the length \(r\).
2282	
2283	  \emph{Base case \(r = 0\).} A length-zero datum has a finiteness witness making the
2284	  subquotient \(N/N'\) a finite-dimensional \(\kappa\)-vector space
2285	  (\cref{lem:graded_finiteDimensional_of_mvPolynomial_isEmpty_finite}); its degreewise
2286	  images form an independent family inside that finite-dimensional space, so only
2287	  finitely many are nonzero and \(\mathrm{hilb}(n) = 0\) for \(n \gg 0\)
2288	  (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_base_eventuallyZero}). By
2289	  \cref{lem:ratHilb_ofEventuallyZero} an eventually-zero function is rational of order
2290	  \(0\), giving \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, 0)\).
2291	
2292	  \emph{Inductive step \(r \ge 1\).} Set \(x = t_{r-1}\). By the kernel/cokernel
2293	  closure (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_ker_coker}) the kernel subquotient
2294	  \(K = (N \cap x^{-1}(N'),\, N')\) and the cokernel subquotient
2295	  \(C = (N,\, N' + x \cdot N)\) are subquotient data of length \(r-1\), assembled by
2296	  the kernel and cokernel constructors (\cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_ker},
2297	  \cref{def:graded_subquotientDatum_coker}) whose finiteness witnesses come from the
2298	  abstract finiteness transfer (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_finite_transfer}). The
2299	  induction hypothesis applied
2300	  to \(K\) and \(C\) gives \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and
2301	  \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\) (\cref{def:ratHilb}). By the degreewise
2302	  difference identity (\cref{lem:graded_subquotient_degreewise_diff}),
2303	  \[
2304	    \mathrm{hilb}(n+1) - \mathrm{hilb}(n) \;=\; h_C(n+1) - h_K(n)
2305	    \qquad \text{for all } n .
2306	  \]
2307	  Feeding \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_C, r-1)\), \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(h_K, r-1)\) and this
2308	  difference identity into the inductive-step engine
2309	  (\cref{lem:ratHilb_ofDiffEq}) yields \(\mathrm{IsRatHilb}(\mathrm{hilb}, r)\). (The
2310	  common order \(r-1\) for \(h_C\) and \(h_K\) is arranged, if necessary, by raising
2311	  the lower one with \cref{lem:ratHilb_bump}.) This completes the induction.
2312	\end{proof}

2314	\section{Support and freeness predicates}
2315	\label{sec:quot_predicates}
2316	
2317	The Quot functor (\cref{def:quot_functor}) and the Grassmannian
2318	(\cref{def:grassmannian_scheme}) classify quotient sheaves subject to two
2319	geometric conditions: a coherent quotient must have \emph{schematic support
2320	proper over the base}, and the Grassmannian's quotients must be \emph{locally
2321	free of a fixed rank}. This section introduces both predicates.
2322	
2323	\begin{definition}
2324	
2325	\leanok
2326	[Annihilator ideal sheaf of a sheaf of modules]
2327	  \label{def:modules_annihilator}

2329	  % NOTE: The definition carries no \uses: the Lean construction is
2330	  % `IdealSheafData.ofIdeals (fun U => Module.annihilator Γ(X,U) Γ(F,U))`, which is well-defined
2331	  % unconditionally and does NOT depend on the localization bridges at definition time. The two
2332	  % facts lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map and lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen are needed
2333	  % only for the downstream CHARACTERIZATION (the reverse inclusion / equality on affine opens, the
2334	  % "annihilator forward direction"), which is not yet a blueprint block.
2335	  % NOTE: The definition itself is formalized and axiom-clean; the inclusion direction
2336	  % \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U) \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\)
2337	  % (\cref{lem:modules_annihilator_ideal_le}) is closed. The reverse inclusion, which identifies
2338	  % the annihilator sheaf with the module-annihilator on affine opens, remains bridge-gated on
2339	  % \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen}.
2340	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "Stratification by Hilbert
2341	  % Polynomials" (read from
2342	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L468-L471).
2343	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
2344	  % "Let $X\to S$ be a finite type morphism of noetherian schemes,

2346	  %  be any coherent sheaf on
2347	  %  $X$ whose schematic support is proper over $S$."
2348	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
2349	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of
2350	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules that is \emph{quasi-coherent and of finite type}
2351	  (over a locally noetherian base, so that on each affine open \(U\) the section
2352	  module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is a finitely generated \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module).
2353	  The \emph{annihilator ideal sheaf} of \(\mathcal{F}\) is the ideal sheaf
2354	  \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\) defined on affine opens: for each affine open
2355	  \(U \subseteq X\),
2356	  \[
2357	    \mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)
2358	    \;=\; \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}\bigl(\mathcal{F}(U)\bigr)
2359	    \;\subseteq\; \mathcal{O}_X(U),
2360	  \]
2361	  the annihilator of the \(\mathcal{O}_X(U)\)-module \(\mathcal{F}(U)\). These
2362	  local data patch to a coherent ideal sheaf on \(X\) whose vanishing locus is
2363	  the set-theoretic support of \(\mathcal{F}\).
2364	
2365	  \emph{Well-definedness on basic opens.} The coherence condition for an ideal
2366	  sheaf requires that, for an affine open \(U\) and \(f \in \mathcal{O}_X(U)\),
2367	  the section \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})(U)\) restricts correctly to the basic
2368	  open \(D(f) \subseteq U\), i.e. that
2369	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(D(f))}(\mathcal{F}(D(f)))\) is the extension of
2370	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U))\) along the localization map
2371	  \(\mathcal{O}_X(U) \to \mathcal{O}_X(D(f))\). This is exactly where the two
2372	  hypotheses enter. The bridge
2373	  \cref{lem:qcoh_section_localization_basicOpen} supplies, for a quasi-coherent
2374	  finite-type \(\mathcal{F}\), that \(\mathcal{O}_X(D(f)) = (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\)
2375	  and that the restriction \(\mathcal{F}(U) \to \mathcal{F}(D(f))\) exhibits
2376	  \(\mathcal{F}(D(f))\) as the localized module \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2377	  \mathcal{F}(U)\); the algebra engine
2378	  \cref{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map} then yields, using that
2379	  \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) is finitely generated, the required identity
2380	  \(\mathrm{Ann}_{(\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f}\bigl((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}
2381	  \mathcal{F}(U)\bigr) = \mathrm{Ann}_{\mathcal{O}_X(U)}(\mathcal{F}(U)) \cdot
2382	  (\mathcal{O}_X(U))_f\). Thus the local annihilators are compatible with basic-open
2383	  restriction, which is the patching datum that makes \(\mathrm{Ann}(\mathcal{F})\)
2384	  a well-defined ideal sheaf. The finite-type hypothesis is genuinely required:
2385	  without finite generation of \(\mathcal{F}(U)\) the annihilator need not commute
2386	  with localization, and the local data fail to patch.
2387	
2388	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not carry an annihilator for sheaves of modules on a scheme.
2389	\end{definition}

2412	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2413	[Annihilator of a finitely generated module commutes with
2414	  localization]
2415	  \label{lem:annihilator_localization_eq_map}
2416	  \lean{Module.annihilator_isLocalizedModule_eq_map}
2417	  Let \(R\) be a commutative ring, let \(S \subseteq R\) be a multiplicative
2418	  submonoid, and let \(R_S = S^{-1}R\) be the localization of \(R\) at \(S\). Let
2419	  \(M\) be a \emph{finitely generated} \(R\)-module and let \(f : M \to M_S\) be
2420	  an \(R\)-linear map exhibiting \(M_S\) as the localized module \(S^{-1}M\)
2421	  (i.e.\ \(f\) is a localization of \(M\) at \(S\)). Then the annihilator of
2422	  \(M_S\) over \(R_S\) is the extension of the annihilator of \(M\) along the
2423	  structure map \(R \to R_S\):
2424	  \[
2425	    \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)
2426	    \;=\; (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S
2427	    \;=\; \mathrm{map}\bigl(R \to R_S\bigr)\bigl(\mathrm{Ann}_R M\bigr).
2428	  \]
2429	  Equivalently, for finitely generated \(M\) the annihilator commutes with
2430	  localization: \(\mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M) = S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M)\).
2431	
2432	  \emph{The finite-generation hypothesis is essential.} For a module that is not
2433	  finitely generated the inclusion \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}(M) \subseteq
2434	  \mathrm{Ann}(S^{-1}M)\) can be strict, so the identity fails. This is the
2435	  standard commutative-algebra fact that the annihilator of a finite module
2436	  localizes; it is recorded here as the load-bearing algebra engine for the
2437	  basic-open coherence of \cref{def:modules_annihilator}.
2438	\end{lemma}
2439	
2440	\begin{proof}
2441	  \(\supseteq\): if \(r \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\), then \(r\) kills every element of
2442	  \(M\), hence its image \(r/1 \in R_S\) kills every element of \(M_S\) (each such
2443	  element is \(m/s = (1/s)\cdot f(m)\) for some \(m \in M\), \(s \in S\), and
2444	  \((r/1)\cdot(m/s) = (1/s)\cdot f(r\,m) = 0\)). Thus
2445	  \((\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S \subseteq \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\).
2446	
2447	  \(\subseteq\): write \(M = \langle m_1, \dots, m_n\rangle\) using the
2448	  finite-generation hypothesis, and let \(y \in \mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S)\). Write
2449	  \(y = a/s\) with \(a \in R\), \(s \in S\). For each generator \(m_i\) the element
2450	  \(y\cdot f(m_i) = (a/s)\cdot f(m_i) = 0\) in \(M_S\); by the definition of the
2451	  localized module this means there is \(u_i \in S\) with \(u_i\,a\,m_i = 0\) in
2452	  \(M\). Put \(u = \prod_{i=1}^n u_i \in S\). Then \(u\,a\) kills every generator
2453	  \(m_i\), hence kills all of \(M\), so \(u\,a \in \mathrm{Ann}_R M\). Finally
2454	  \(y = a/s = (u\,a)/(u\,s)\) lies in \(S^{-1}\,\mathrm{Ann}_R M =
2455	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\). Therefore \(\mathrm{Ann}_{R_S}(M_S) \subseteq
2456	  (\mathrm{Ann}_R M)\cdot R_S\), and combined with the reverse inclusion the two
2457	  ideals coincide. The use of a single common multiplier \(u = \prod_i u_i\) is
2458	  exactly the step that requires finitely many generators.
2459	\end{proof}

1937	\begin{lemma}[Conjugation distributes over composition (reassoc simp lemma)]
1938	  \label{lem:conjugateEquiv_comp_mathlib}
1939	  \lean{CategoryTheory.conjugateEquiv_comp}
1940	  \mathlibok
1941	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
1942	  Let \(\mathrm{adj}_1 : L_1 \dashv R_1\), \(\mathrm{adj}_2 : L_2 \dashv R_2\),
1943	  \(\mathrm{adj}_3 : L_3 \dashv R_3\) be adjunctions between the same pair of categories, and let
1944	  \(\alpha : L_2 \Rightarrow L_1\), \(\beta : L_3 \Rightarrow L_2\) be transformations of left adjoints.
1945	  Then the conjugates compose, with order reversed onto the right-adjoint side:
1946	  \[
1947	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_2)\,\alpha \;\circ\;
1948	    \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_2,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,\beta
1949	      \;=\; \operatorname{conj}(\mathrm{adj}_1,\mathrm{adj}_3)\,(\beta \circ \alpha) .
1950	  \]
1951	  Tagged \(\mathtt{@[reassoc (attr := simp)]}\), it is the lead member of the conjugate
1952	  reassociation simp set: it fuses the per-adjunction conjugates of a composite adjunction into the
1953	  conjugate of the composite (the conjugate-side form of the paste-of-mates).
1954	\end{lemma}

2406	  map on restriction of scalars (their section-level values are the identity) — and they
2407	  serve only to rebracket the iterated pushforward along the two factorizations
2408	  \((g' \circ f)_* = (f' \circ g)_*\) of the square's composite. (The
2409	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{g',f})^{\mathrm{hom}}\) collapse is a transparent re-association
2410	  that is most naturally accounted for together with the leg-reindex of step~(iii) rather than at
2411	  this point; the \(\Gamma\)-collapse identity itself is the same.) The congruence factor collapses
2412	  to a canonical transport along the equality of opens: its section-level value
2413	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\,w)^{\mathrm{hom}}\) is the
2414	  presheaf-restriction along the canonical equality of opens, i.e.\ a repackaging along
2415	  that equality carrying no substantive content. Thus after the \(\Gamma\)-collapse the only
2416	  surviving non-trivial factor is \(f_{*}\bigl(\eta^{g'}_{\widetilde M}\bigr)\) read on global
2417	  sections.
2418	
2419	  \emph{(iii) Reduction to Seam~1 via the leg-reindex engine.} With the legs substituted
2420	  (step~(i)) to \(g' = e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), the leg-reindex engine
2421	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pullbackPushforward_unit_comp}, instantiated at \(a = e\),
2422	  \(b = \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A\), \(N = \widetilde M\), rewrites the unit factor
2423	  \(\eta^{g'} = \eta^{e \circ \operatorname{Spec}\iota_A}\), together with the
2424	  \((\mathrm{pushforwardComp}_{e,\,\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A})\) coherence, into the affine
2425	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit followed by \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)_{*}\) of the
2426	  \(e\)-unit. Since \(e\) is an isomorphism of schemes, its pullback functor is an equivalence
2427	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:pullback_isEquivalence_of_iso}) and the \(e\)-unit is invertible, hence an
2428	  isomorphism absorbed into the codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\). The surviving
2429	  \((\operatorname{Spec}\iota_A)\)-unit has section value over \(\operatorname{Spec} A\) equal to
2430	  the algebraic unit \(\eta_M : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\) of Seam~1
2431	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_unit_value}). Composing the surviving factor with the
2432	  codomain identification \(\Theta_{\mathrm{tgt}}\)
2433	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_codomain_read}) and reading over \(\operatorname{Spec} R\) by
2434	  restriction of scalars along \(\psi\) identifies all four factors with the restriction of
2435	  scalars along \(\psi\) of the codomain-read transport of the unit value, namely
2436	  \(\rho : m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\)
2437	  (Definition~\ref{def:base_change_mate_inner_value}) — the
2438	  ``\(X' = \operatorname{Spec}(R' \otimes_R A)\), \(\mathcal F'\) is
2439	  \((R' \otimes_R A) \otimes_A M\)'' bookkeeping of the source. Each of (i)--(iii) extracts as a
2440	  named lemma; together they close the reindex without ever rewriting the bare legs.
2441	\end{proof}

2511	      of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\)
2512	      (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}); and
2513	    \item the section-restriction map
2514	      \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}\bigl(D(f)\bigr)\) exhibits
2515	      \(\mathcal{M}(D(f))\) as the localized module
2516	      \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\mathcal{M}(U)\); that is, it is a localization
2517	      of the \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(\mathcal{M}(U)\) at the submonoid
2518	      \(\mathrm{powers}\,f = \{1, f, f^2, \dots\}\).
2519	  \end{enumerate}
2520	\end{lemma}

2522	\begin{proof}
2523	  \uses{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
2524	  Part (1) is \cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}, the Mathlib statement
2525	  that on an affine open \(U\) the sections over the basic open \(D(f)\) form the
2526	  localization of \(\Gamma(X,U)\) away from \(f\).
2527	
2528	  For part (2), identify the affine open \(U\) with \(\Spec \Gamma(X, U)\). Over
2529	  this affine, a quasi-coherent sheaf is \(\widetilde{N}\) for the
2530	  \(\Gamma(X,U)\)-module \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\), the sheaf associated to
2531	  \(N\) (no finiteness of \(N\) is required for this step). The Spec-local
2532	  computation of the sections of \(\widetilde{N}\) on a
2533	  basic open gives \(\Gamma\bigl(D(f), \widetilde{N}\bigr) = N_f\) as an
2534	  \((\Gamma(X,U))_f\)-module, and the restriction map
2535	  \(\Gamma(\Spec \Gamma(X,U), \widetilde{N}) = N \to N_f\) is precisely the
2536	  localization homomorphism \(N \to (\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1} N\). Transporting
2537	  this identification across the affine isomorphism
2538	  \(U \cong \Spec \Gamma(X, U)\) -- under which \(\mathcal{M}|_U\) corresponds to
2539	  \(\widetilde{N}\) by quasi-coherence and the basic open \(D(f) \subseteq U\)
2540	  corresponds to the standard open \(D(f) \subseteq \Spec\Gamma(X,U)\) -- shows
2541	  that the restriction \(\mathcal{M}(U) \to \mathcal{M}(D(f))\) is the
2542	  localization of \(N = \mathcal{M}(U)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\), i.e.\ it is a
2543	  localized module at \(\mathrm{powers}\,f\). The substantive content is this
2544	  transport of the Spec-local localization fact for the sheaf \(\widetilde{N}\)
2545	  to a general quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{M}\) via the affine identification.

2762	  on every basic open, so G1-core and gap1 are interderivable and the genuine content sits entirely

2768	\begin{lemma}[Quasi-coherence = a cover with local presentations]
2769	  \label{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}
2770	  \lean{SheafOfModules.QuasicoherentData}
2771	  \mathlibok
2772	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2773	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.Quasicoherent}).}
2774	  A sheaf of modules \(M\) on a scheme is \emph{quasi-coherent}
2775	  (\(\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}\,M\)) exactly when the type of \emph{quasi-coherence data}
2776	  \(\mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\,M\) is nonempty. A datum
2777	  \(q : \mathrm{QuasicoherentData}\,M\) consists of an index family \(\{X_i\}\) of opens covering the
2778	  whole scheme together with, for each \(i\), a presentation
2779	  \((M.\mathrm{over}\,X_i).\mathrm{Presentation}\) of the restriction of \(M\) to \(X_i\)
2780	  (a cokernel-of-free-modules description on the site over \(X_i\)). Thus quasi-coherence packages a
2781	  cover on which \(M\) is locally presented; on each member of the cover
2782	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_of_presentation_mathlib} applies.
2783	\end{lemma}

2787	\begin{lemma}[Pullback of sheaves of modules along a morphism of schemes]
2788	  \label{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}
2789	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.pullback}
2790	  \mathlibok
2791	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.Sheaf}).}
2792	  For a morphism of schemes \(f : X \to Y\), Mathlib supplies the pullback functor
2793	  \(f^{*} : Y.\mathrm{Modules} \to X.\mathrm{Modules}\) on sheaves of modules, left adjoint to
2794	  pushforward, hence colimit-preserving, and carrying the unit sheaf to the unit sheaf. The project
2795	  already uses this functor in the definition of local freeness. It is the geometric restriction
2796	  through which a presentation is transported to an open subscheme.
2797	\end{lemma}

2799	\begin{lemma}[Restriction of sheaves of modules along an open immersion]

2812	  \label{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}

2852	\begin{lemma}[Localization is flat]
2853	  \label{lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib}
2854	  \lean{IsLocalization.flat}
2855	  \mathlibok
2856	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.RingTheory.Flat.Localization}).}
2857	  If \(S\) is a localization of a commutative ring \(R\) at a multiplicative submonoid, then \(S\) is
2858	  a flat \(R\)-module. Consequently localization is exact and commutes with finite limits; this is
2859	  what lets the section-localization functor pass through the finite sheaf equalizer in the descent
2860	  (\cref{lem:section_localization_descent}).
2861	\end{lemma}

2863	% --- The finite basic-open cover (topological precursor) ---
2864	
2865	\begin{lemma}
2866	
2867	\leanok
2868	[Finite basic-open cover refining a quasi-coherence cover]
2869	  \label{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}
2870	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}
2871	  \uses{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib, lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}
2872	  \textit{The topological finite-cover front of the gap1 transport: any quasi-coherence cover refines
2873	  to a finite basic-open cover.}
2874	  Let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) and let \(q\) be quasi-coherence data for \(M\)
2875	  (\cref{lem:isQuasicoherent_quasicoherentData_mathlib}): a — possibly infinite — open cover
2876	  \(\{q.X_i\}\) of \(\top\) together with a presentation of \(M.\mathrm{over}\,(q.X_i)\) on each
2877	  member. Then there is a \emph{finite} subset \(t \subseteq R\) whose basic opens cover \(\Spec R\)
2878	  (equivalently \(\mathrm{span}\,t = (1)\)) such that each \(D(r)\), \(r \in t\), is contained in some
2879	  member \(q.X_i\) of the presentation cover.
2880	\end{lemma}
2881	\begin{proof}
2882	  \uses{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}
2883	  The set \(G = \{\,r \in R : D(r) \le q.X_i \text{ for some } i\,\}\) spans the unit ideal. Indeed
2884	  every prime of \(R\) lies in some member \(q.X_i\) by the covering property \(q.\mathrm{coversTop}\)
2885	  (read off the \(\mathrm{Opens}\) Grothendieck-topology sieve), and the basic opens form a basis of
2886	  the Zariski topology (\cref{lem:isBasis_basic_opens_mathlib}), so a basic open \(D(r) \subseteq
2887	  q.X_i\) is a neighbourhood of that prime; hence \(\bigsqcup_{r \in G} D(r) = \top\), which is to say
2888	  \(\mathrm{span}\,G = (1)\). A unit-ideal membership uses only finitely many of the generators,
2889	  yielding a finite \(t \subseteq G\) with \(\mathrm{span}\,t = (1)\) and each \(D(r) \le q.X_i\) for
2890	  \(r \in t\).
2891	\end{proof}

2902	\begin{lemma}[Slice of opens equivalent to opens of the open subspace]
2903	  \label{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}

2905	  \mathlibok
2906	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.Topology.Sheaves.Over}).}
2907	  For a topological space \(X\) and an open \(U \subseteq X\), the slice category of opens of
2908	  \(X\) over \(U\) (the opens of \(X\) contained in \(U\), with inclusions) is equivalent to the
2909	  opens of the subspace \(U\):
2910	  \((\mathrm{Opens}\,X)_{/U} \simeq \mathrm{Opens}(U)\). Mathlib supplies this underlying
2911	  equivalence of sites; it leaves the lift to sheaf categories as an explicit TODO.

2914	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf categories of equivalent sites are equivalent]
2915	  \label{lem:equivalence_sheafCongr_mathlib}
2916	  \lean{CategoryTheory.Equivalence.sheafCongr}
2917	  \mathlibok
2918	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib (\texttt{Mathlib.CategoryTheory.Sites.Equivalence}).}
2919	  Let \(e : C \simeq D\) be an equivalence of categories carrying Grothendieck topologies \(J\)
2920	  on \(C\) and \(K\) on \(D\), and suppose \(e.\mathrm{inverse}\) is a dense subsite for
2921	  \((K, J)\). Then for any target category \(A\) the sheaf categories are equivalent:
2922	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}(J, A) \simeq \mathrm{Sheaf}(K, A)\).
2923	\end{lemma}

2929	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
2930	  (\texttt{Mathlib.Algebra.Category.ModuleCat.Sheaf.PushforwardContinuous}).}
2931	  Let \(e : C \simeq D\) be an equivalence of sites with both \(e.\mathrm{functor}\) and
2932	  \(e.\mathrm{inverse}\) continuous, and let \(S\) (a sheaf of rings on \(J\)) and \(R\) (a sheaf
2933	  of rings on \(K\)) be related by mutually inverse comparison maps across the continuous
2934	  pushforwards. Then the categories of sheaves of modules are equivalent,
2935	  \(\mathrm{SheafOfModules}\,R \simeq \mathrm{SheafOfModules}\,S\), compatibly with the

2937	  \cref{lem:over_restrict_iso}.

2940	\begin{lemma}\leanok
2941	[The over-equivalence functor is cocontinuous]
2942	  \label{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous}
2943	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous}
2944	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2945	  \textit{Topological layer of bridge C; fills the \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean} TODO.}
2946	  The functor of \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib})
2947	  is cocontinuous (cover-lifting) from the \(U\)-slice \(J_X|_U\) of the opens Grothendieck
2948	  topology of \(X\) to the opens Grothendieck topology \(J_U\) of the subspace \(U\).
2949	\end{lemma}
2950	\begin{proof}
2951	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2952	  A sieve covers in the slice topology \(J_X|_U\) iff its image sieve covers in \(J_X\), and
2953	  covering for the opens topology is pointwise: a sieve covers iff its members' opens cover the
2954	  base set-theoretically. Given a point \(x\) of a slice object and a cover member in \(J_U\)
2955	  containing the image of \(x\), transport it across the open embedding \(U \hookrightarrow X\),
2956	  which is injective: the set-theoretic image of an open \(V \subseteq U\) is an open of \(X\)
2957	  contained in the base, a neighbourhood of \(x\) lying in the image sieve. This witnesses the
2958	  cover-lifting condition.
2959	\end{proof}

2965	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}

2967	  Symmetrically, the inverse of \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) is cocontinuous from the
2968	  opens topology \(J_U\) of the subspace \(U\) to the \(U\)-slice \(J_X|_U\).
2969	\end{lemma}
2970	\begin{proof}
2971	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
2972	  As in \cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isCocontinuous}, reduce to the pointwise covering
2973	  criterion and the over-sieve image criterion; given a point and a slice-cover member, pull the
2974	  member back along the embedding \(U \hookrightarrow X\) (preimage of an open of \(X\) is an open
2975	  of the subspace) to obtain a cover member of \(J_U\) witnessing the lift.
2976	\end{proof}

3014	\begin{definition}\leanok

3016	  \label{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}

3018	  \uses{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib, lem:equivalence_sheafCongr_mathlib,

3020	  \textit{Keystone of the topological layer of bridge C.}
3021	  For any category \(A\), the site equivalence \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\)
3022	  (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}) lifts to an equivalence of sheaf categories
3023	  \[
3024	    \mathrm{Sheaf}(J_X|_U,\, A) \;\simeq\; \mathrm{Sheaf}(J_U,\, A),
3025	  \]
3026	  obtained by feeding the dense-subsite witness
3027	  (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isDenseSubsite}) to the general sheaf-congruence
3028	  construction (\cref{lem:equivalence_sheafCongr_mathlib}). This is exactly the equivalence left
3029	  as a TODO in Mathlib's \texttt{Topology/Sheaves/Over.lean}, and it is the object across which

3033	% --- (C, step 3) The module-level equivalence and its functor identification ---
3034	
3035	% The two blocks below are the module-category layer assembled on top of the topological
3036	% sheaf-congruence def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr: the step-3 equivalence of categories of sheaves of
3037	% modules (def:over_restrict_equiv) and the step-4 functor identification (lem:over_restrict_functor_iso)
3038	% whose object component is the slice-touching bridge lem:over_restrict_iso below. They are
3039	% project-bespoke infrastructure (no external source).
3040	
3041	\begin{definition}
3042	
3043	\leanok
3044	[The slice-to-geometric module-category equivalence (gap1, C, step 3)]
3045	  \label{def:over_restrict_equiv}
3046	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictEquiv}
3047	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3048	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3049	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}
3050	  \textit{Module-level lift of the topological bridge C; the step-3 equivalence on which the
3051	  slice-touching isomorphism is built.}
3052	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open. The category of sheaves of modules over the
3053	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) on the slice site \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U\) is
3054	  equivalent to the category \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{Modules}\) of sheaves of modules on the open
3055	  subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\):
3056	  \[
3057	    \mathrm{SheafOfModules}\bigl(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\bigr)
3058	      \;\simeq\; U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{Modules}.
3059	  \]
3060	  This is the module-category lift of the topological sheaf equivalence
3061	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}.
3062	\end{definition}
3063	\begin{proof}
3064	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3065	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3066	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}
3067	  Apply the module-sheaf transport \(\mathrm{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\)
3068	  (\cref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}) to the site equivalence
3069	  \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}), whose two legs are
3070	  both continuous (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous},
3071	  \cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}). The transport consumes a pair of mutually inverse
3072	  ring-sheaf comparison morphisms between the sliced structure sheaf
3073	  \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) and the geometric structure sheaf
3074	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U.\mathrm{toScheme}}\); for these one takes the whiskered (co)unit of the site
3075	  equivalence \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) into the structure presheaf, paired with the
3076	  identity. The identity is legal precisely because the geometric structure sheaf is
3077	  the transport of the sliced one across the ring-valued instance of
3078	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr} (this is step~2 of the bridge, which holds by
3079	  construction). Orienting the resulting equivalence so that its functor runs from the slice
3080	  side to the geometric side gives the stated equivalence.
3081	\end{proof}

3083	\begin{lemma}

3085	\leanok
3086	[Functor identification of the slice-to-geometric equivalence (gap1, C, step 4)]
3087	  \label{lem:over_restrict_functor_iso}

3089	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,

3091	  \textit{The composite of the slice functor with the step-3 equivalence is the geometric restriction
3092	  functor.}
3093	  With \(X\) and \(U \subseteq X\) as above, the composite of the abstract Grothendieck-slice functor
3094	  \(M \mapsto M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) (pushforward along the identity of the sliced ring sheaf) with the
3095	  functor of the step-3 equivalence (\cref{def:over_restrict_equiv}) is naturally isomorphic to the
3096	  geometric restriction functor along the open immersion \(U.\iota\):
3097	  \[
3098	    \bigl(\mathrm{pushforward}\,\mathbf{1}\bigr) \;\circ\; (\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,U).\mathrm{functor}
3099	      \;\cong\; \mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota
3100	  \]
3101	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}).

3105	    lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}
3106	  Both sides are pushforwards of sheaves of modules along the \emph{same} opens functor. Indeed the
3107	  opens functor of the open immersion factors as
3108	  \[
3109	    U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}
3110	      \;=\; (\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U).\mathrm{inverse}\;\circ\;\mathrm{Over.forget}\,U
3111	  \]
3112	  (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}), an equality that holds by construction, and
3113	  \(\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota\) (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}) is precisely
3114	  pushforward along \(U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}\). The left-hand composite is pushforward along the
3115	  factored functor on the right. Identify the two via the composition law for module-sheaf
3116	  pushforward (\(\mathrm{pushforwardComp}\)) followed by congruence of pushforward along the equal ring
3117	  morphisms (\(\mathrm{pushforwardCongr}\)); the required ring-morphism equality is routine.

3122	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3123	[The Grothendieck-slice restriction equals the geometric restriction (gap1, C)]
3124	  \label{lem:over_restrict_iso}
3125	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictIso}
3126	  \uses{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib,
3127	    lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib, def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr,
3128	    lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous, lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous,
3129	    lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib, lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}
3130	  % NOTE: RESOLVED and axiom-clean. AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictIso
3131	  % now exists (#print axioms = {propext, Classical.choice, Quot.sound}); gap1 bridge C
3132	  % (steps 1->4) is fully closed. The step-2 geometric ring-sheaf identification collapsed to
3133	  % `rfl` (U.toScheme.ringCatSheaf is definitionally the transport of the sliced sheaf, via
3134	  % toScheme_presheaf_obj/map). The statement is routed through the step-3 equivalence functor:
3135	  % overRestrictIso : (overRestrictEquiv U).functor.obj (M.over U) ≅ (restrictFunctor U.ι).obj M.
3136	  \textit{The one lemma that must touch the Grothendieck slice site; everything downstream of it is
3137	  geometric.}
3138	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(U \subseteq X\) an open. The \emph{abstract Grothendieck-slice}
3139	  restriction \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) — an object of the category of sheaves of modules over the
3140	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) on the slice site \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U\) —
3141	  is canonically isomorphic to the \emph{geometric} restriction
3142	  \((\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota).\mathrm{obj}\,M\)
3143	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}), equivalently to the pullback \(U.\iota^{*} M\)
3144	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}), as a sheaf of modules on the small Zariski
3145	  site of the open subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\). The isomorphism is induced by the equivalence
3146	  of sites \(J.\mathrm{over}\,U \simeq \mathrm{Opens}(U.\mathrm{toScheme})\) coming from the opens
3147	  functor of the open immersion \(U.\iota\), under which the sliced sheaf of rings
3148	  \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) corresponds to the structure sheaf of \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\).
3149	  For \(X = \Spec R\) and \(U = D(r)\) a basic open, the target category is
3150	  \((\Spec R_r).\mathrm{Modules}\) under the affine identification \(D(r) \cong \Spec R_r\)
3151	  (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}).
3152	\end{lemma}
3153	\begin{proof}
3154	  \uses{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr, lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous,
3155	    lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous, lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib,
3156	    lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3157	    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}
3158	  The isomorphism is built in four steps, ascending from the purely topological layer to the
3159	  module-level statement.
3160	
3161	  \emph{Step 1 (topological layer --- done).} The opens functor of the open immersion
3162	  \(U.\iota\) realizes the slice of the opens site of \(X\) over \(U\) as the opens site of the
3163	  subspace \(U\); on sheaf categories this is the equivalence
3164	  \(\mathrm{Sheaf}(J_X|_U,\,A) \simeq \mathrm{Sheaf}(J_U,\,A)\) of
3165	  \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}, for every target category \(A\). This is the
3166	  topos-theoretic layer, assembled from the cocontinuity/continuity of both legs of
3167	  \(\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U\) (\cref{lem:opens_overEquivalence_mathlib}).
3168	
3169	  \emph{Step 2 (geometric ring-sheaf identification --- the current obstacle).} Identify the
3170	  sliced structure sheaf \(\mathcal{O}_X.\mathrm{over}\,U\) with the structure sheaf
3171	  \(\mathcal{O}_{U}\) of the open subscheme \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}\), transported across the
3172	  \(\mathrm{RingCat}\)-valued instance of \cref{def:overEquivalence_sheafCongr}; this is an
3173	  isomorphism of sheaves of rings. The bridge is the factorization of the opens functor of the
3174	  immersion,
3175	  \[
3176	    U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}
3177	      \;=\; (\mathrm{Opens.overEquivalence}\,U).\mathrm{inverse}
3178	        \;\circ\; \mathrm{Over.forget}\,U ,
3179	  \]
3180	  under which the structure-sheaf comparison reduces to the standard open-immersion
3181	  structure-sheaf identifications; recall that \(\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota\)
3182	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}) is precisely pushforward along
3183	  \(U.\iota.\mathrm{opensFunctor}\). This ring-sheaf identification is the remaining geometric
3184	  obstacle.
3185	
3186	  \emph{Step 3 (lift to modules).} Given the step-2 identification of the underlying ring sheaves
3187	  and the continuity of both legs (\cref{lem:overEquivalence_functor_isContinuous},
3188	  \cref{lem:overEquivalence_inverse_isContinuous}), lift the sheaf-of-rings comparison to an
3189	  equivalence of categories of sheaves of modules via
3190	  \(\mathrm{pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence}\)
3191	  (\cref{lem:pushforwardPushforwardEquivalence_mathlib}). Under this equivalence the sliced
3192	  module \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U\) corresponds to the geometric restriction
3193	  \((\mathrm{restrictFunctor}\,U.\iota).\mathrm{obj}\,M\).
3194	
3195	  \emph{Step 4 (compose).} Compose with
3196	  \(\mathrm{restrictFunctorIsoPullback}\) (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib})
3197	  to land the isomorphism \(M.\mathrm{over}\,U \cong M.\mathrm{restrict}\,U.\iota \cong
3198	  U.\iota^{*}M\). The two sides live a priori in different module categories, so the literal Lean
3199	  statement of \(\mathrm{overRestrictIso}\) is phrased through the step-3 equivalence functor; for
3200	  \(X = \Spec R\) and \(U = D(r)\) the target is \((\Spec R_r).\mathrm{Modules}\) under
3201	  \(D(r) \cong \Spec R_r\) (\cref{lem:isLocalization_basicOpen_mathlib}).
3202	\end{proof}

3244	\begin{definition}\leanok
3245	[Step-3 equivalence sends the unit module to the unit module]
3246	  \label{def:over_restrict_unit_iso}
3247	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.overRestrictUnitIso}
3248	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv}
3249	  \textit{The step-3 slice equivalence functor preserves the structure-sheaf (unit) module.}
3250	  Let \(U \subseteq X\) be an open subscheme. The functor of the step-3 slice
3251	  equivalence (\cref{def:over_restrict_equiv}) carries the unit
3252	  (structure-sheaf) module \(\mathbf{1}\) of the over-site
3253	  \(X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{over}\,U\) to the unit module of
3254	  \(U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}\):
3255	  \[
3256	    (\mathrm{overRestrictEquiv}\,U).\mathrm{functor}.\mathrm{obj}\,
3257	      \bigl(\mathbf{1}\,(X.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}.\mathrm{over}\,U)\bigr)
3258	      \;\cong\; \mathbf{1}\,(U.\mathrm{toScheme}.\mathrm{ringCatSheaf}) .
3259	  \]
3260	\end{definition}
3261	\begin{proof}
3262	  \uses{def:over_restrict_equiv}
3263	  By construction the functor of the equivalence is the pushforward
3264	  \(\mathrm{pushforward}\,\psi_0\) of sheaves of modules along the
3265	  over-site/open-site comparison, where \(\psi_0 = \mathrm{homMk}(\mathbf{1})\)
3266	  is the identity ring comparison. The canonical unit comparison
3267	  \(\mathrm{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\,\psi_0 : \mathbf{1}_S \to
3268	  (\mathrm{pushforward}\,\psi_0).\mathrm{obj}\,\mathbf{1}_R\) is an isomorphism
3269	  whenever the underlying ring comparison \(\psi_0\) is one: reflecting through the
3270	  forgetful passage to presheaves (\(\mathrm{toSheaf}/\mathrm{sheafToPresheaf}\)),
3271	  its component over each object \(V\) is the functor-image of
3272	  \(\psi_0.\mathrm{app}\,V\), hence an isomorphism (this \(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\psi\)
3273	  driven criterion is supplied by the auxiliary
3274	  \(\mathrm{isIso\_unitToPushforwardObjUnit\_of\_isIso}'\)). Here
3275	  \(\psi_0 = \mathrm{homMk}(\mathbf{1})\) is the identity, so the unit comparison
3276	  is an isomorphism, and its inverse is the asserted isomorphism. (Mathlib's
3277	  isomorphism statement for \(\mathrm{unitToPushforwardObjUnit}\) instead requires
3278	  a finality hypothesis on the comparison functor; the \(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\psi\)
3279	  driven form used here is the one the slice equivalence needs.)
3280	\end{proof}

2876	[(B) Generator close: the base change of $\rho$ is the inverse regrouping]
2877	  \label{lem:base_change_mate_gstar_generator_close}

2885	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, Lemma ``Affine base change''
2886	  (proof, ``boils down to the equality'' step).}
2887	  Let \(\rho : M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\), \(m \mapsto (1 \otimes 1) \otimes m\), be the
2888	  inner comparison value of Definition~\ref{def:base_change_mate_inner_value}. The extension of
2889	  scalars along \(\psi : R \to R'\) of \(\rho\), post-composed with the algebraic counit
2890	  \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) of the extension/restriction-of-scalars adjunction along \(\psi\),
2891	  is an \(R'\)-linear map \(R' \otimes_R M \to (A \otimes_R R') \otimes_A M\); on the generator
2892	  \(r' \otimes m\) it returns \(r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) = (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\),
2893	  which is exactly the value of the inverse regrouping isomorphism
2894	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv}) on \(r' \otimes m\). Both maps being
2895	  \(R'\)-linear and agreeing on the generators \(r' \otimes m\), they coincide:
2896	  \[
2897	    \operatorname{ext}_\psi(\rho) \,\circ\, \varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}
2898	      \;=\; \operatorname{regroup}^{-1}.
2899	  \]
2900	\end{lemma}
2901	\begin{proof}
2902	  \uses{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv, def:base_change_mate_inner_value}
2903	  Both sides are \(R'\)-linear maps out of \(R' \otimes_R M\), so by extensionality it suffices to
2904	  check the value on the generators \(r' \otimes m\). The left side sends
2905	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto r' \cdot \rho(m) = r' \cdot ((1 \otimes 1) \otimes m) =
2906	  (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\), using that \(\varepsilon^{\mathrm{alg}}\) reads the extension of
2907	  scalars by acting through the \(R'\)-scalar. The inverse regrouping isomorphism of
2908	  Lemma~\ref{lem:base_change_mate_regroupEquiv} sends, by definition,
2909	  \(r' \otimes m \mapsto (1 \otimes r') \otimes m\) (in the \(A \otimes_R R'\) orientation). The two
2910	  values agree on every generator, so the maps are equal. No flatness hypothesis is used.
2911	\end{proof}

3645	  \emph{\(\mathrm{exists\_of\_eq}\) (a vanishing fraction is annihilated by a power of \(f\)).}
3646	  Let \(x \in \Gamma(M, \top)\) with \(\theta(x) = x|_{D(f)} = 0\). On each cover member \(D(r)\), the
3647	  restriction \(x|_{D(r)}\) maps to \(0\) in \(\Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(r))\), so by the localization
3648	  hypothesis (the case \(U = D(r)\) of \(\mathrm{Hfr}\), which gives the \(\mathrm{exists\_of\_eq}\)
3649	  field on that piece) there is \(n_r\) with \(f^{n_r}\, x|_{D(r)} = 0\). A common exponent
3650	  \(N \ge \max_r n_r\) over the finite cover gives \(f^{N} x|_{D(r)} = 0\) for every \(r\); since the
3651	  \(D(r)\) cover \(\Spec R\), separatedness (\cref{lem:eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}) forces
3652	  \(f^{N} x = 0\) in \(\Gamma(M, \top)\).
3653	
3654	  These three fields are exactly \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) for \(\theta\).
3655	  Equivalently the comparison \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is an
3656	  isomorphism -- the \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})\) of Stacks
3657	  \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections} on \(X = \Spec R\), \(X_f = D(f)\), obtained from local data over a
3658	  finite cover by sheaf gluing and separatedness, with the finite limit interacting with localization
3659	  through the flatness of localization (\cref{lem:isLocalization_flat_mathlib},
3660	  \cref{lem:isLimitPullbackCone_mathlib}), never through the global affine equivalence.
3661	\end{proof}

3665	\begin{lemma}[Global sections of a pullback along an open immersion are sections over the image
3666	  (gap1, Hfr section transport)]
3667	  \label{lem:pullback_gamma_top_iso}
3668	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Modules.gammaPullbackTopIso}
3669	  \uses{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3670	    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib, lem:over_restrict_pullback_iso,
3671	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
3672	    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ}
3673	  % NOTE: the pinned Lean decl gammaPullbackTopIso does NOT yet exist; it is the iter-036 prover
3674	  % target — the SECTION analogue of P1's OBJECT-level transport
3675	  % (\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent}), Mathlib-absent at the pinned commit.
3676	  % Once it lands, the named-form descent (\cref{lem:section_localization_descent}) and gap1
3677	  % (\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}) are both one-liners. Project-bespoke infrastructure:
3678	  % no external verbatim source for the construction; it stands on its sketch.
3679	  \textit{Project-bespoke infrastructure: the section-level transport feeding the per-cover-element
3680	  hypothesis \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) of \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover}.}
3681	  Let \(j : U \hookrightarrow X\) be an open immersion of schemes and let \(M\) be a sheaf of modules
3682	  on \(X\). The global sections of the pullback module along \(j\) are canonically isomorphic to the
3683	  sections of \(M\) over the image of \(j\):
3684	  \[
3685	    \Gamma\bigl((\mathrm{pullback}\,j).\mathrm{obj}\,M,\ \top\bigr)
3686	    \;\cong\; \Gamma\bigl(M,\ \mathrm{range}\,j\bigr),
3687	  \]
3688	  naturally in the open argument: for opens \(V \le \top\) of \(U\) the comparison
3689	  \(\Gamma((\mathrm{pullback}\,j).\mathrm{obj}\,M, V) \cong \Gamma(M, j(V))\) intertwines the
3690	  restriction maps of the two presheaves, so any two such instances commute with the restriction
3691	  between them.
3692	\end{lemma}
3693	
3694	\begin{proof}
3695	  \uses{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib, lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib,
3696	    lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib}
3697	  For an open immersion \(j\), Mathlib's restriction functor \(j^{\mathrm{res}}\) satisfies
3698	  \(\Gamma(j^{\mathrm{res}} M, V) = \Gamma(M, j(V))\) \emph{definitionally}
3699	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctor_mathlib}); in particular at \(V = \top\) the right-hand side is
3700	  \(\Gamma(M, j(\top)) = \Gamma(M, \mathrm{range}\,j)\). The restriction functor is naturally
3701	  isomorphic to the universal-property pullback,
3702	  \(j^{\mathrm{res}} \cong j^{*} = \mathrm{pullback}\,j\)
3703	  (\cref{lem:modules_restrictFunctorIsoPullback_mathlib},
3704	  \cref{lem:modules_pullback_mathlib}). Applying the global-sections functor \(\Gamma(-, V)\) to the
3705	  component of this natural isomorphism at \(M\) gives, for each open \(V\), an isomorphism
3706	  \(\Gamma((\mathrm{pullback}\,j).\mathrm{obj}\,M, V) \cong \Gamma(j^{\mathrm{res}} M, V)
3707	  = \Gamma(M, j(V))\); naturality of the functor isomorphism in \(V\) makes these commute with the
3708	  presheaf restriction maps. Taking \(V = \top\) yields the displayed isomorphism with
3709	  \(\Gamma(M, \mathrm{range}\,j)\).
3710	\end{proof}

3768	  \textit{Source: [Stacks Project], "Properties of Schemes", \S ``Sections over principal opens'',
3769	  Lemma \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections}; cf.\ [Hartshorne], II.5.3.}
3770	  Let \(M\) be a quasi-coherent sheaf of modules on \(\Spec R\) (equivalently, by
3771	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent} and
3772	  \cref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}, \(M\) is locally isomorphic to a
3773	  tilde \(\widetilde{N_j}\) on the members of a finite basic-open cover \(\{D(r_j)\}\) of
3774	  \(\Spec R\)). Then for every \(f \in R\) the section restriction
3775	  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) exhibits the target as the localized module
3776	  \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\Gamma(M, \top)\); that is, it is
3777	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over \(R\).

3779	% NOTE: the lemma STATEMENT is cited to Stacks lemma-invert-f-sections / Hartshorne II.5.3; the proof
3780	% below is a project-internal descent (finite sheaf equalizer over the cover + flatness of
3781	% localization) that deliberately does NOT route through the global affine equivalence (which is
3782	% gap1 itself), so there is no transcribed `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF`.
3783	\begin{proof}
3784	  \uses{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover, lem:pullback_gamma_top_iso,
3785	    lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent,
3786	    lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData,
3787	    lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ,
3788	    lem:isLocalizedModule_tilde_restrict}
3789	  Fix \(f \in R\). Refine the quasi-coherence cover to a finite basic-open cover
3790	  \(\{D(r)\}_{r \in t}\) of \(\Spec R\), \(\mathrm{span}(t) = (1)\), with each \(D(r) \le q.X_{i(r)}\)
3791	  (\cref{lem:exists_finite_basicOpen_cover_le_quasicoherentData}). It suffices to produce the
3792	  per-cover-element localization data \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) of
3793	  \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover}: for every open \(U \le D(r)\) (\(r \in t\)) the
3794	  restriction \(\Gamma(M, U) \to \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap U)\) is
3795	  \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\). The keystone then assembles the three fields of
3796	  the conclusion for the global restriction \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\).
3797	
3798	  We obtain \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) at \(U \le D(r)\) from the per-element P1 transport. By
3799	  \cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_basicOpen_of_quasicoherent} the module \(M'\) obtained by restricting
3800	  \(M\) to \(D(r)\) -- realised as the iterated pullback of \(M\) along \(\iota_{q.X_i}\), the
3801	  basic-open immersion, and the affine identification \(D(r) \cong \Spec R_r\) -- has an isomorphism
3802	  \(\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\) counit, so by
3803	  \cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ} its section restriction to any basic open
3804	  of \(\Spec R_r\) is a localization. The section transport \cref{lem:pullback_gamma_top_iso}, chained
3805	  through the same three pullbacks, identifies \(\Gamma(M', \top) \cong \Gamma(M, D(r))\) and, at the
3806	  \(f\)-locus, \(\Gamma(M', D(f')) \cong \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap D(r))\) (with \(f'\) the image of \(f\)
3807	  in \(R_r\)), intertwining the restriction maps; the same for an overlap \(U = D(r) \sqcap D(r')\).
3808	  Transporting the localization statement across these section isomorphisms gives that
3809	  \(\Gamma(M, U) \to \Gamma(M, D(f) \sqcap U)\) is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\),
3810	  which is exactly \(\mathrm{Hfr}\).
3811	
3812	  Feeding \(\mathrm{Hfr}\) and the cover \(\{D(r)\}_{r\in t}\) to
3813	  \cref{lem:section_localization_descent_of_cover} yields that
3814	  \(\Gamma(M, \top) \to \Gamma(M, D(f))\) is \(\mathrm{IsLocalizedModule}(\mathrm{powers}\,f)\) over
3815	  \(R\), i.e.\ \((\mathrm{powers}\,f)^{-1}\,\Gamma(M, \top) \cong \Gamma(M, D(f))\). This is the
3816	  \(\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \cong \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})\) of Stacks
3817	  \texttt{lemma-invert-f-sections} on \(X = \Spec R\), \(X_f = D(f)\), obtained from local-tilde data
3818	  over a finite cover without appeal to the global affine equivalence.
3819	\end{proof}

3862	\subsection{G1-assemble: from per-basic-open section localization to the
3863	\texorpdfstring{$\widetilde{(-)}$}{tilde}-counit}
3864	
3865	This subsection records the bridge \(\text{section-localization on every basic open} \Rightarrow
3866	\mathrm{IsIso}\,M.\mathrm{fromTildeΓ}\): given that the section restriction localizes on every basic
3867	open, the tilde-Gamma counit is an isomorphism. Together with its converse
3868	\cref{lem:isLocalizedModule_restrict_of_isIso_fromTildeΓ} it assembles the equivalence
3869	\cref{lem:isIso_fromTildeΓ_iff_isLocalizedModule_restrict}, which is exactly what makes G1-core
3870	(\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_section_localization}) and gap1
3871	(\cref{lem:qcoh_affine_isIso_fromTildeΓ}) interderivable -- the route by which G1-core is obtained as
3872	a corollary of gap1. The supporting Mathlib facts are recorded as dependency anchors.

4065	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
4066	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}

4071	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
4072	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
4073	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
4074	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
4075	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
4076	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.
4077	\end{lemma}

2145	\section{Sections over principal opens}
2146	\label{section-principal-opens}
2147	
2148	\noindent
2149	Here is a typical result of this kind. We will use a more naive but
2150	more direct method of proof in later lemmas.
2151	
2152	\begin{lemma}
2153	\label{lemma-invert-f-sections}
2154	\begin{slogan}
2155	Sections of quasi-coherent sheaves have only meromorphic singularities
2156	at infinity.
2157	\end{slogan}
2158	Let $X$ be a scheme. Let $f \in \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$.
2159	Denote $X_f \subset X$ the open where $f$ is invertible, see
2160	Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-f-open}.
2161	If $X$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated, the canonical map
2162	$$
2163	\Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)_f \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{O}_X)
2164	$$
2165	is an isomorphism. Moreover, if $\mathcal{F}$ is a quasi-coherent
2166	sheaf of $\mathcal{O}_X$-modules the map
2167	$$
2168	\Gamma(X, \mathcal{F})_f \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_f, \mathcal{F})
2169	$$
2170	is an isomorphism.
2171	\end{lemma}
2172	
2173	\begin{proof}
2174	Write $R = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X)$. Consider the canonical morphism
2175	$$
2176	\varphi : X \longrightarrow \Spec(R)
2177	$$
2178	of schemes, see
2179	Schemes, Lemma
2180	\ref{schemes-lemma-morphism-into-affine}.
2181	Then the inverse image of the standard open $D(f)$ on the
2182	right hand side is $X_f$ on the left hand side.
2183	Moreover, since $X$ is assumed quasi-compact and quasi-separated
2184	the morphism $\varphi$ is quasi-compact and quasi-separated,
2185	see Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-quasi-compact-affine} and
2186	\ref{schemes-lemma-compose-after-separated}. Hence by
2187	Schemes, Lemma \ref{schemes-lemma-push-forward-quasi-coherent}
2188	we see that $\varphi_*\mathcal{F}$ is quasi-coherent.
2189	Hence we see that $\varphi_*\mathcal{F} = \widetilde M$

3477	\section{Flat base change for the pushforward}
3478	
3479	The global statement is assembled from the affine lemma and the single homological
3480	input that flatness preserves the finite equalizer computing \(H^0\). That input is
3481	supplied verbatim by Mathlib, recorded here as a dependency anchor.
