3483	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
3484	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3485	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
3486	  \mathlibok
3487	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3488	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat
3489	  \(A\)-module that is also an \(A\)-algebra, with the scalar-tower compatibility),
3490	  and let \(f, g : N \rightrightarrows P\) be a pair of \(A\)-linear maps. Write
3491	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
3492	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
3493	  \[
3494	    B \otimes_A \operatorname{eq}(f, g)
3495	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;
3496	    \operatorname{eq}\bigl(\mathbf{1}_B \otimes f,\ \mathbf{1}_B \otimes g\bigr)
3497	  \]
3498	  is an isomorphism; that is, base change along a flat algebra commutes with the
3499	  formation of the finite equalizer (equivalently, of the kernel: the case
3500	  \(g = 0\) is \texttt{LinearMap.tensorKerEquiv}, underlain by
3501	  \texttt{Module.Flat.ker\_lTensor\_eq} and \texttt{Module.Flat.eqLocus\_lTensor\_eq}).
3502	  Since \(- \otimes_A B\) also preserves finite products, it follows that
3503	  \(- \otimes_A B\) carries the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) equalizer
3504	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
3505	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
3506	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.
3507	\end{lemma}

3516	  opens with union \(U = \bigcup_i U_i\). Then \(\mathcal{F}\) satisfies the sheaf

3532	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3533	[Finite affine cover with quasi-compact overlaps]
3534	  \label{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact}
3536	  A quasi-compact scheme \(X\) admits a \emph{finite} affine open cover
3537	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\). If moreover \(X\) is
3538	  quasi-separated, then each pairwise intersection \(U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j\) is
3539	  quasi-compact; and if \(X\) is separated, each \(U_{ij}\) is in fact affine.
3540	  \begin{proof}
3541	    The affine opens form a basis, so \(X\) is covered by affine opens; quasi-compactness
3542	    extracts a finite subcover \(U_1, \dots, U_t\) (by Mathlib's affine cover and the
3543	    finite-subcover property of a quasi-compact space). Quasi-separatedness says the
3544	    intersection of two quasi-compact opens is quasi-compact, so each \(U_{ij}\) is
3545	    quasi-compact. When \(X\) is separated the diagonal is a closed immersion, whence
3546	    the intersection of two affine opens is affine (Mathlib, for separated schemes).
3547	  \end{proof}
3548	\end{lemma}

4065	\begin{lemma}[Properness as a morphism property]
4066	  \label{lem:isProper_mathlib}

4068	  \mathlibok
4069	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib
4070	  (\texttt{Mathlib.AlgebraicGeometry.Morphisms.Proper}).}
4071	  A morphism \(f : X \to S\) of schemes is \emph{proper} iff it is separated,
4072	  universally closed, and locally of finite type. Properness is stable under base
4073	  change: if \(f : X \to S\) is proper and \(g : T \to S\) is any morphism then
4074	  the base change \(X \times_S T \to T\) is proper. Consequently the base-change
4075	  clause that Nitsure invokes (``properness \ldots\ preserved by base-change'')
4076	  applies directly to the proper-support condition below.

4098	  Let \(\mathcal{F}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
4099	  \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism. We say \(\mathcal{F}\) \emph{has proper support
4100	  over \(S\) (along \(f\))} when the composite morphism
4101	  \[
4102	    \mathrm{schematicSupport}(\mathcal{F}) \hookrightarrow X \xrightarrow{f} S
4103	  \]
4104	  is proper (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}). Since properness is stable under base
4105	  change (\cref{lem:isProper_mathlib}), this condition is preserved by pull-back
4106	  along any \(S'\to S\), which is exactly what the well-definedness of the Quot
4107	  functor's pullback action requires.
4108	\end{definition}

4110	\begin{definition}
4111	
4112	\leanok
4113	[Locally free of rank \(d\)]
4114	  \label{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
4115	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.SheafOfModules.IsLocallyFreeOfRank}
4116	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises (2)" and
4117	  % "Grassmannian of a vector bundle" (read from
4118	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L562-L564
4119	  % + L584-L585).
4120	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4121	  % "$\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients $q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F$
4122	  %  where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$.
4123	  %  [...]
4124	  %  the set of all equivalence classes $\langle \F, q\rangle$ of quotients
4125	  %  $q: E_T \to \F$ where $\F$ is a locally free sheaf on $T$ of rank $d$,"
4126	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
4127	  Let \(\mathcal{M}\) be a sheaf of modules on a scheme \(X\) and let
4128	  \(d \in \mathbb{N}\). We say \(\mathcal{M}\) is \emph{locally free of rank
4129	  \(d\)} when there exists an open cover \(\{U_i\}\) of \(X\) together with
4130	  isomorphisms \(\mathcal{M}|_{U_i} \cong \mathcal{O}_{U_i}^{\oplus d}\) for
4131	  each \(i\).
4132	
4133	  \emph{Note.} Mathlib does not provide this predicate.
4134	\end{definition}

4136	\section{The Quot functor}
4137	\label{sec:quot_functor}
4138	
4139	\begin{definition}\leanok
4140	[Quot functor]
4141	  \label{def:quot_functor}
4142	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.QuotFunctor}
4143	  \uses{def:hilbert_polynomial, def:has_proper_support}
4144	
4145	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, sub-section "The Functors \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) and
4146	  % \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\)" (read from
4147	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L394-L433
4148	  % + L481-L498).
4149	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4150	  % "Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(X\to S\) be a finite type
4151	  %  scheme over it. Let \(E\) be a coherent sheaf on \(X\).
4152	  %  Let \(Sch_S\) denote the category of all locally noetherian schemes
4153	  %  over \(S\). For any \(T\to S\) in \(Sch_S\), a \textbf{family of quotients of \(E\)
4154	  %  parametrised by \(T\)} will mean
4155	  %  a pair \((\F,q)\) consisting of
4156	  %
4157	  %  (i) a coherent sheaf \(\F\) on \(X_T = X\times_ST\)
4158	  %  such that the schematic support of \(\F\) is proper over \(T\)
4159	  %  and \(\F\) is flat over \(T\), together with
4160	  %
4161	  %  (ii) a surjective \({\mathcal O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism of sheaves
4162	  %  \(q:E_T  \to \F\) where \(E_T\) is the pull-back of \(E\) under
4163	  %  the projection \(X_T \to X\).
4164	  %
4165	  %  Two such families \((\F,q)\) and \((\F,q)\) parametrised by \(T\) will
4166	  %  be regarded as equivalent if \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\)),
4167	  %  and \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) will denote
4168	  %  an equivalence class. Then as properness and flatness are preserved
4169	  %  by base-change, and as tensor-product is right exact,
4170	  %  the pull-back of \(\langle \F, q \rangle\) under an \(S\)-morphism \(T'\to T\) is
4171	  %  well-defined, which gives
4172	  %  a set-valued contravariant functor
4173	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S} : Sch_S \to Sets\) under which
4174	  %  \(\)T\mapsto  \{\mbox{ All } \langle \F, q \rangle \mbox{ parametrised by }T\}\(\)
4175	  %  [...]
4176	  %  This shows that the functor \(\Quot_{E/X/S}\) naturally decomposes
4177	  %  as a co-product
4178	  %  \(\)\Quot_{E/X/S} = \coprod_{{\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]}\, \Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\(\)
4179	  %  where for any polynomial \({\Phi}\in \Q[\lambda]\), the functor
4180	  %  \(\Quot_{E/X/S}^{\Phi,L}\) associates
4181	  %  to any \(T\) the set of all equivalence classes of
4182	  %  families \(\langle \F,q \rangle\) such that at each \(t\in T\) the
4183	  %  Hilbert polynomial of the restriction \(\F_t\), calculated
4184	  %  using the pull-back of \(L\), is \({\Phi}\)."
4185	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1 (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
4186	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme, \(\pi : X \to S\) a finite-type morphism,
4187	  \(L\) a line bundle on \(X\), \(E\) a coherent sheaf on \(X\), and
4188	  \(\Phi \in \mathbb{Q}[\lambda]\). The \emph{Quot functor with Hilbert
4189	  polynomial \(\Phi\)}
4190	  \[
4191	    \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S} : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set}
4192	  \]
4193	  sends an \(S\)-scheme \(T \to S\) to the set of equivalence classes
4194	  \(\langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle\) of pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) where
4195	  \begin{enumerate}
4196	    \item \(\mathcal{F}\) is a coherent sheaf on \(X_T = X \times_S T\) whose
4197	      schematic support is proper over \(T\) (\cref{def:has_proper_support}) and
4198	      which is flat over \(T\);
4199	    \item \(q : E_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) is a surjective
4200	      \(\mathcal{O}_{X_T}\)-linear homomorphism, where \(E_T\) is the pull-back
4201	      of \(E\) along \(X_T \to X\);
4202	    \item for every \(t \in T\) the Hilbert polynomial of \(\mathcal{F}|_{X_t}\)
4203	      with respect to \(L|_{X_t}\) (\cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}) equals
4204	      \(\Phi\).
4205	  \end{enumerate}
4206	  Two pairs \((\mathcal{F}, q)\) and \((\mathcal{F}', q')\) are equivalent iff
4207	  \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\). On morphisms \(f : T' \to T\) over \(S\), the functor
4208	  acts by pulling back the quotient along \(X_{T'} \to X_T\) (well-defined
4209	  since tensor product is right-exact and preserves both flatness and
4210	  properness). The Hilbert scheme is the special case
4211	  \(\Hilb^{\Phi,L}_{X/S} := \Quot^{\Phi,L}_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\).
4212	\end{definition}

4214	\section{The Grassmannian scheme}
4215	\label{sec:grassmannian_scheme}
4216	
4217	The Quot construction proceeds by embedding \(\Quot^{\Phi,L}_{E/X/S}\) as a
4218	locally closed subfunctor of a Grassmannian over \(S\). Since Mathlib carries
4219	no Grassmannian \emph{scheme} (only a finite-rank-quotient variety in
4220	linear algebra), we package the construction here.
4221	
4222	\begin{definition}\leanok
4223	[Grassmannian scheme]
4224	  \label{def:grassmannian_scheme}
4225	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.Grassmannian}
4226	  \uses{def:quot_functor, def:is_locally_free_of_rank}
4227	  % SOURCE: [Nitsure], \S 1, "Elementary Examples, Exercises~(2)" and
4228	  % "Construction of Grassmannian" (read from
4229	  % references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex, L546-L599
4230	  % + L773-L850).
4231	  % SOURCE QUOTE:
4232	  % "For any integers \(r\ge d\ge 1\), an explicit construction
4233	  %  the Grassmannian scheme
4234	  %  \(\grass(r,d)\) over \(\Z\), together with the tautological
4235	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \
4236	  %  \(\U\) is a rank \(d\) locally free sheaf on \(\grass(r,d)\),
4237	  %  has been given at the end of this section. [...]
4238	  %  Show that \(\grass(r,d)\) together with the quotient
4239	  %  \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) represents the
4240	  %  contravariant functor
4241	  %  \(\)\Grass(r,d) = \Quot_{\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\Z}/\Z/\Z}^{d, {\mathcal O}_{\Z}}\(\)
4242	  %  from schemes to sets, which associates to any \(T\) the
4243	  %  set of all equivalence classes
4244	  %  \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients \(q: \oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_T \to \F\)
4245	  %  where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\).
4246	  %  [...]
4247	  %  Using the above show that, more generally, if \(S\) is a scheme and
4248	  %  \(E\) is a locally free \({\mathcal O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\),
4249	  %  the functor \(\Grass(E,d)=\Quot_{E/S/S}^{d,{\mathcal O}_S}\)
4250	  %  on all \(S\)-schemes which
4251	  %  by definition associates to any \(T\)
4252	  %  the set of all equivalence classes \(\langle \F, q\rangle\) of quotients
4253	  %  \(q: E_T \to \F\) where \(\F\) is a locally free sheaf on \(T\) of rank \(d\),
4254	  %  is representable. The representing scheme
4255	  %  is denoted by \(\grass(E,d)\) and is called the rank \(d\)
4256	  %  relative Grassmannian of \(E\) over \(S\)."
4257	  \textit{Source: [Nitsure], \S 1, Exercise (2) and "Construction of
4258	  Grassmannian" (FGA Explained Ch.~5).}
4259	  Let \(S\) be a noetherian scheme and let \(V\) be a locally free
4260	  \(\mathcal{O}_S\)-module of rank \(r\). For an integer \(1 \le d \le r\), the
4261	  \emph{Grassmannian of rank-\(d\) quotients of \(V\)} is the functor
4262	  \[
4263	    \mathrm{Grass}(V, d) : (\mathrm{Sch}/S)^{op} \longrightarrow \mathbf{Set},
4264	    \qquad
4265	    T \longmapsto \{ \langle \mathcal{F}, q\rangle :
4266	    q : V_T \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F},\; \mathcal{F}
4267	    \text{ locally free of rank } d \}
4268	  \]
4269	  with equivalence \(\ker(q) = \ker(q')\); the rank-\(d\) local freeness of
4270	  \(\mathcal{F}\) is as in \cref{def:is_locally_free_of_rank}. Concretely,
4271	  \(\mathrm{Grass}(V, d) = \Quot^{d,\mathcal{O}_S}_{V/S/S}\): the Quot functor
4272	  (\cref{def:quot_functor}) in the special case \(X = S\), \(E = V\), constant
4273	  Hilbert polynomial \(\Phi = d\).
4274	\end{definition}

4316	  %  quotient \(u:\oplus^r\,{\mathcal O}_{\grass(r,d)} \to \U\) where \

4346	  of rank \(r\), and \(1 \le d \le r\). The functor \(\mathrm{Grass}(V,d)\) of
4347	  \cref{def:grassmannian_scheme} is representable by a smooth projective
4348	  \(S\)-scheme \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d) \to S\) of relative dimension \(d(r-d)\),
4349	  equipped with a tautological quotient
4350	  \(\pi^* V \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) of rank \(d\). The determinant
4351	  line bundle \(\det(\mathcal{U})\) is relatively very ample, and the
4352	  associated linear system gives a closed embedding

4358	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: see the "Construction of Grassmannian" sub-section of
4359	% [Nitsure], \S 1 (references/nitsure-hilbert-quot-src/nitsure-hilbert-quot.tex,
4360	% L773-L930), which gives the construction in five named steps
4361	% ("Construction by gluing together affine patches", "Separatedness",
4362	% "Properness", "Universal quotient", "Projective embedding"). The total
4363	% verbatim text is ~150 lines; it is reproduced piecewise in the
4364	% SOURCE QUOTE comment of the theorem statement above.
4365	\begin{proof}
4366	  
4367	  Over \(S = \Spec \mathbb{Z}\) and \(V = \mathcal{O}_S^{\oplus r}\) the
4368	  Grassmannian \(\mathrm{Gr}(r, d)\) is constructed by gluing
4369	  \(\binom{r}{d}\) affine charts \(U^I = \Spec\mathbb{Z}[X^I]\) indexed by
4370	  size-\(d\) subsets \(I \subseteq \{1, \dots, r\}\). The chart \(U^I\)
4371	  carries the matrix \(X^I\) of size \(d \times r\) whose \(I\)-th \(d \times d\)
4372	  minor is the identity, with the remaining \(d(r-d)\) entries free
4373	  variables (so \(U^I \cong \mathbb{A}^{d(r-d)}_{\mathbb{Z}}\)). The
4374	  transition maps \(\theta_{I,J} : U^I \cap U^J \to U^J \cap U^I\) defined
4375	  by \(X^J \mapsto (X^I_J)^{-1} X^I\) on the locus where \(\det X^I_J\) is
4376	  invertible satisfy the cocycle condition
4377	  \(\theta_{I,K} = \theta_{I,J} \circ \theta_{J,K}\). The glued
4378	  \(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d) \to \Spec\mathbb{Z}\) is smooth of relative dimension
4379	  \(d(r-d)\). Separatedness is checked from the diagonal
4380	  \(\Delta_{I,J} \subset U^I \times U^J\) cut out by \(X^J_I X^I - X^J = 0\).
4381	  Properness follows from the valuative criterion for DVRs: given
4382	  \(\varphi : \Spec K \to \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\) over a DVR \(R\) with fraction
4383	  field \(K\), factoring through some chart \(U^I\) via a ring map
4384	  \(f : \mathbb{Z}[X^I] \to K\), choose \(J\) minimising the valuation
4385	  \(\nu(f(P^I_J))\) of the minor; on the chart \(U^J\) all entries have
4386	  non-negative valuation, so \(f\) extends uniquely to \(\Spec R\).
4387	
4388	  The universal quotient \(u : \mathcal{O}_{\mathrm{Gr}(r,d)}^{\oplus r}
4389	  \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{U}\) is given on \(U^I\) by the matrix \(X^I\)
4390	  and glued by \(g_{I,J} = (X^I_J)^{-1} \in GL_d(U^I \cap U^J)\). The
4391	  determinant line bundle \(\det\mathcal{U}\) has transition functions
4392	  \(\det g_{I,J} = 1/P^I_J\); the global sections
4393	  \(\sigma_I \in \Gamma(\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), \det\mathcal{U})\) defined by
4394	  \(\sigma_I|_{U^J} = P^J_I\) form a base-point-free linear system separating
4395	  points over \(\Spec\mathbb{Z}\), giving an embedding into
4396	  \(\mathbb{P}^{\binom{r}{d}-1}_{\mathbb{Z}}\), which is closed by
4397	  properness. Representability of \(\mathrm{Grass}(r,d)\) is then verified
4398	  on the universal pair \((\mathrm{Gr}(r,d), u)\): a quotient
4399	  \(q : \mathcal{O}_T^{\oplus r} \twoheadrightarrow \mathcal{F}\) of
4400	  rank \(d\) on \(T\) is everywhere locally trivialised; on the locus where
4401	  the columns indexed by \(I\) are a basis the matrix of \(q\) has the form
4402	  \(X^I\), defining the classifying morphism \(T \to U^I \subset
4403	  \mathrm{Gr}(r,d)\). Base change from \(\mathbb{Z}\) to a general noetherian
4404	  base \(S\) with a vector bundle \(V\) is direct: trivialise \(V\) on an open
4405	  cover and glue. For non-trivial \(V\) the same construction produces
4406	  \(\mathrm{Gr}_S(V, d)\) with Pl\"ucker embedding into
4407	  \(\mathbb{P}_S(\bigwedge^d V)\).
4408	\end{proof}

4410	\section{Out of scope}
4411	\label{sec:quot_out_of_scope}
4412	
4413	This chapter packages \emph{only} the Quot-foundations layer: the Hilbert
4414	polynomial, the Quot functor, and the Grassmannian scheme with its
4415	representability. The following are explicitly out of scope here:
4416	\begin{itemize}
4417	  \item \textbf{Representability of the Quot scheme} -- the
4418	    Grothendieck--Altman--Kleiman existence theorem and its proof skeleton
4419	    (boundedness, Grassmannian embedding, flattening stratification,
4420	    valuative criterion) are not constructed in this chapter.
4421	  \item \textbf{Flat base change of cohomology} (Stacks 02KH) and its
4422	    associated prerequisites -- not constructed in this chapter.
4423	  \item \textbf{Hilbert scheme as a separate chapter}. The Hilbert scheme
4424	    \(\Hilb_{X/S}\) is the special case
4425	    \(\Hilb_{X/S} = \Quot_{\mathcal{O}_X/X/S}\) of the Quot functor and is
4426	    introduced inline (\cref{def:quot_functor}); no separate chapter.
4427	  \item \textbf{Snapper's Lemma} -- the polynomial-eventually property of
4428	    Euler characteristics, used in \cref{def:hilbert_polynomial}. Cited
4429	    via Nitsure \S 1 and (textbook reference) Hartshorne III.5.2;
4430	    Lean formalization is a prerequisite sub-task.
4431	  \item \textbf{Identification with \(\mathbb{P}^n\) and relative
4432	    Grassmannian variants} (Nitsure \S 1, Exercises (1)--(4)) --
4433	    optional applications, not consumed by Route A.
4434	  \item \textbf{Group-scheme structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)} (A.2.c) -- the
4435	    abelian-group-scheme refinement is downstream. Its blueprint home is the
4436	    FGA Picard-representability assembly (the chapter that bundles the relative
4437	    Picard functor, its representability, and the abelian-group-scheme
4438	    structure on \(\Pic_{C/k}\)); that assembly lives in the parent project
4439	    which consumes this Quot-foundations layer and is not part of the present
4440	    subproject.
4441	  \item \textbf{Identity-component \(\Pic^0\) and abelian-variety
4442	    structure} (A.3) -- a separate Route A sub-row, fed by but not
4443	    constructed in this chapter.
4444	\end{itemize}

3483	\begin{lemma}[Flat base change preserves finite equalizers]
3484	  \label{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}
3485	  \lean{LinearMap.tensorEqLocusEquiv}
3486	  \mathlibok
3487	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3488	  Let \(A \to B\) be a flat ring homomorphism (more generally, let \(B\) be a flat

3491	  \(\operatorname{eq}(f, g) = \{x \in N : f(x) = g(x)\}\) for their equalizer. Then the
3492	  canonical \(B\)-linear comparison map
3493	  \[

3495	    \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\;

3497	  \]

3504	  \(\prod_i \Gamma(U_i, \mathcal{F}) \rightrightarrows
3505	    \prod_{i,j} \Gamma(U_{ij}, \mathcal{F})\) of a finite cover to the corresponding
3506	  equalizer of the base-changed cover.

3532	\begin{lemma}\leanok
3533	[Finite affine cover with quasi-compact overlaps]
3534	  \label{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}
3535	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Scheme.exists_finite_affineCover_inter_isQuasiCompact}
3536	  A quasi-compact scheme \(X\) admits a \emph{finite} affine open cover
3537	  \(\mathcal{U} : X = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_t\). If moreover \(X\) is
3538	  quasi-separated, then each pairwise intersection \(U_{ij} = U_i \cap U_j\) is
3539	  quasi-compact; and if \(X\) is separated, each \(U_{ij}\) is in fact affine.
3540	  \begin{proof}
3541	    The affine opens form a basis, so \(X\) is covered by affine opens; quasi-compactness
3542	    extracts a finite subcover \(U_1, \dots, U_t\) (by Mathlib's affine cover and the
3543	    finite-subcover property of a quasi-compact space). Quasi-separatedness says the
3544	    intersection of two quasi-compact opens is quasi-compact, so each \(U_{ij}\) is
3545	    quasi-compact. When \(X\) is separated the diagonal is a closed immersion, whence
3546	    the intersection of two affine opens is affine (Mathlib, for separated schemes).
3547	  \end{proof}
3548	\end{lemma}

3733	    (Lemma~\ref{lem:finite_affine_cover_qcqs}) and induct on \(t\). For \(t = 1\),
3734	    \(X = U_1\) is affine and the claim is the affine lemma

3736	    (affine) and \(W = U_1 \cup \dots \cup U_{t-1}\) (covered by \(t-1\) affines, hence

3836	  with \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module and
3837	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). Assume that \(g\) is flat and that \(f\)
3838	  is quasi-compact and quasi-separated. Then the base-change map of
3839	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism
3840	  \[
3841	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
3842	    f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr) = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
3843	  \]
3844	  Equivalently, in the affine situation \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
3845	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the comparison map
3846	  \[
3847	    H^0(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\;\sim\;}\;
3848	    H^0(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B)
3849	  \]
3850	  is an isomorphism, where \(X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)}
3851	  \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) and \(\mathcal{F}_B\) is the pullback of
3852	  \(\mathcal{F}\).
3853	
3854	  The hypothesis that \(\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent is essential: it is what
3855	  makes \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) quasi-coherent on the affine base and thus the tilde of
3856	  a module, and it is what lets the affine reduction
3857	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}) write \(\mathcal{F}\) as
3858	  \(\widetilde{M}\) over each affine open. Without it the comparison map need not be
3859	  an isomorphism.
3860	\end{theorem}

3932	  For \(i = 0\) the argument is {\v C}ech-\emph{free}: it uses only the sheaf
3933	  condition (the degree-\(0\)/\(1\) part of {\v C}ech, i.e.\ an equalizer of a finite
3934	  cover), the affine lemma, and the fact that flat base change commutes with that
3935	  finite equalizer. There is no Leray sequence and no {\v C}ech-to-cohomology spectral
3936	  sequence. The proof is the composite of the decomposition chain.
3937	
3938	  \medskip
3939	  \emph{Reduction to the global-sections comparison.} By
3940	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_reduce_global_sections}, the base-change map of
3941	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map} is an isomorphism if and only if,
3942	  after reducing to affine bases \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
3943	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(B)\) with \(A \to B\) flat, the module comparison map
3944	  \[
3945	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \longrightarrow \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B),
3946	    \qquad X_B = X \times_{\operatorname{Spec}(A)} \operatorname{Spec}(B),
3947	  \]
3948	  is an isomorphism (this uses that \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) is quasi-coherent — \(f\) is
3949	  quasi-compact and quasi-separated — so each side is the tilde of its module of
3950	  global sections, and that pullback along \(g\) is \(- \otimes_A B\) on modules).
3951	
3952	  \medskip
3953	  \emph{The comparison map is an isomorphism.} Since \(f\) is quasi-compact and
3954	  quasi-separated, \(X\) is quasi-compact and quasi-separated over \(A\), so
3955	  Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_mayer_vietoris} applies and gives
3956	  \[
3957	    \Gamma(X, \mathcal{F}) \otimes_A B \;\xrightarrow{\ \sim\ }\; \Gamma(X_B, \mathcal{F}_B).
3958	  \]
3959	  Internally that lemma presents \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{F})\) as the finite
3960	  sheaf-condition equalizer of a finite affine cover
3961	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:gamma_finite_equalizer}), base changes the equalizer diagram term by
3962	  term via the affine lemma Lemma~\ref{lem:affine_base_change_pushforward}
3963	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:base_changed_equalizer_diagram}), and commutes \(- \otimes_A B\)
3964	  past that finite equalizer by flatness
3965	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}), reducing the general
3966	  quasi-separated case to the separated case
3967	  (Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_base_change_separated}) by Mayer--Vietoris induction on the
3968	  number of cover members. Combined with the reduction above, the sheaf-level
3969	  base-change map is an isomorphism.
3970	\end{proof}

3972	\subsection{The \texorpdfstring{$A$}{A}-module \texorpdfstring{$\operatorname{eqLocus}$}{eqLocus} globalization (FBC-B)}
3973	
3974	The separated-case argument is repackaged here at the level of \(A\)-modules, where
3975	\(A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X)\) is the ring of global sections of the structure sheaf, so that flat
3976	base change becomes the Mathlib equivalence \(\operatorname{tensorEqLocusEquiv}\)
3977	(Lemma~\ref{lem:flat_preserves_equalizer_mathlib}) applied to an honest
3978	\(\operatorname{LinearMap.eqLocus}\). The blocks below construct that presentation concretely: every
3979	module of sections \(\Gamma(U, M)\) is viewed as an \(A\)-module by restriction of scalars along the
3980	structure-sheaf restriction \(A \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\), the restriction maps of \(M\) become
3981	\(A\)-linear, and the two product legs of the sheaf condition assemble into a pair of \(A\)-linear maps
3982	whose equalizer locus is \(\Gamma(X, M)\). Throughout, \(M : X.\mathrm{Modules}\) is a sheaf of
3983	\(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules and \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) is an open cover with
3984	\(\bigvee_i U_i = \top\).
3985	
3986	The bijectivity of the keystone presentation rests on two element-level sheaf axioms of the underlying
3987	abelian-group sheaf \(M.\mathrm{presheaf}\), recorded as Mathlib dependency anchors.
3988	
3989	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf separatedness: local equality forces global equality]
3990	  \label{lem:sheaf_eq_of_locally_eq_mathlib}
3991	  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.eq_of_locally_eq'}
3992	  \mathlibok
3993	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
3994	  Let \(\mathcal{G}\) be a sheaf on a topological space \(X\), \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) a
3995	  family covering an open \(V\) (i.e.\ \(V \le \bigvee_i U_i\)), and \(s, t \in \mathcal{G}(V)\) two
3996	  sections. If the restrictions of \(s\) and \(t\) to every \(U_i\) agree, then \(s = t\). This is the
3997	  separatedness half of the sheaf condition, stated element-wise.
3998	\end{lemma}
3999	
4000	\begin{lemma}[Sheaf gluing: a compatible family has a unique amalgamation]
4001	  \label{lem:sheaf_existsUnique_gluing_mathlib}
4002	  \lean{TopCat.Sheaf.existsUnique_gluing'}
4003	  \mathlibok
4004	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
4005	  Let \(\mathcal{G}\) be a sheaf on \(X\), \(U : \iota \to \mathrm{Opens}(X)\) a family covering an open
4006	  \(V\), and \((s_i)_i\) a family of sections \(s_i \in \mathcal{G}(U_i)\) that is \emph{compatible}
4007	  (the restrictions of \(s_i\) and \(s_j\) to \(U_i \cap U_j\) agree for all \(i, j\)). Then there is a
4008	  unique \(s \in \mathcal{G}(V)\) whose restriction to each \(U_i\) is \(s_i\). This is the gluing half
4009	  of the sheaf condition, stated element-wise.
4010	\end{lemma}

4012	\begin{definition}\leanok
4013	[The ground ring of global sections]
4014	  \label{def:fbcb_groundRing}
4015	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.groundRing}
4016	  For a scheme \(X\), the \emph{ground ring} is the commutative ring
4017	  \(A = \Gamma(X, \mathcal{O}_X) = \mathcal{O}_X(\top)\) of global sections of the structure sheaf,
4018	  taken from the \(\mathbf{CommRing}\)-valued structure presheaf so its ring and algebra structures
4019	  resolve canonically.

4022	\begin{definition}\leanok
4023	[The structure-sheaf restriction ring map \texorpdfstring{$A \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)$}{A to sections over U}]
4024	  \label{def:fbcb_rhoU}
4025	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rhoU}
4026	  \uses{def:fbcb_groundRing}
4027	  For an open \(U \subseteq X\), the structure-sheaf restriction along \(U \subseteq \top\) is a ring
4028	  homomorphism \(\rho_U : A = \Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X)\). It is the ring map
4029	  along which sections over \(U\) are regarded as \(A\)-modules.
4030	\end{definition}

4032	\begin{lemma}\leanok
4033	[Transitivity of the restriction ring maps]
4034	  \label{lem:fbcb_rhoU_comp}
4035	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.rhoU_comp}
4036	  \uses{def:fbcb_rhoU}
4037	  For opens \(V \le U \subseteq X\), the structure-sheaf restriction
4038	  \(\Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\) composed with \(\rho_U\) equals \(\rho_V\):
4039	  \[
4040	    \bigl(\Gamma(U,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\bigr) \circ \rho_U = \rho_V .
4041	  \]
4042	  \begin{proof}
4043	    \uses{def:fbcb_rhoU}
4044	    Both sides are the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(X,\mathcal{O}_X) \to \Gamma(V,\mathcal{O}_X)\)
4045	    along \(V \subseteq \top\), factored as \(V \subseteq U \subseteq \top\); functoriality of the
4046	    structure presheaf collapses the two-step restriction to the one-step restriction.
4047	  \end{proof}
4048	\end{lemma}
